day 2 in lisbon ✨ portugal lxfactory leonetta lisbon travelvlog  explore a magical day in lisbon filled with delicious food, stunning sights, and memorable experiences. discover all the highlights! portugal travelvlog lisbon lisbon, the enchanting capital of portugal, offers an incredible blend of culture, food, and unforgettable sights. on this perfect day, we start our adventure with a magical hotel breakfast that sets the tone for the rest of the day. freshly squeezed fruit juices, homemade jams, delectable pastries, and the aroma of warm bread greet us each morning. the highlight of breakfast was undoubtedly the luscious omelette, accompanied by bread slathered in butter and jam, plus bite-sized brownie delights. this was truly a slice of paradise.  our day takes us to a remarkable monastery, a must-visit historical site. despite having tickets, we encounter an astonishingly long line, but we make the best of it by grabbing refreshing iced lattes from a nearby coffee shop. the wait, about 50 minutes, proves worthwhile as we step into an experience resembling a blend of castle grandeur and the magical halls of hogwarts—an absolute must for any visitor to lisbon.  after our time at the monastery, we stroll across the street to admire a giant and visually stunning monument. it's impossible to resist indulging in one of lisbon's iconic pastries, the pastel de nata, from a place renowned for its delectable treats since 1837. we savor each bite while enjoying wine and appetizers, taking a moment to cool down.  next on our adventure is the lx factory, a charming street brimming with diverse restaurants and shops. as we wander through an iconic bookstore, we marvel at its breathtaking atmosphere. shopping here is an extraordinary experience, where i find myself wanting to buy everything! a delightful discovery awaits as austin uncovers chocolate salami, a unique dessert that we can't resist trying; we share three different slices because it's simply that good.  exploring the second level of shops unveils even more treasures, including a gorgeous shell necklace that i can't leave behind. with our bags full, we head back towards our hotel, making a stop at a beautiful wine bar where the ceiling is adorned with wine bottles. it’s hot outside; i find myself guzzling water in between sips of wine, happily sharing my drink with austin.  after a quick change at the hotel, i proudly wear my new necklace as we stroll to our dinner reservation at leonetta. the atmosphere, the vibe, everything about this place is stunning. austin, known for his critical taste, surprises me by giving our meal a perfect 10 out of 10! the burrata appetizer was a standout, making me wish i could have it every day.  as we conclude our evening with a delightful dinner date, it's evident that this perfect day in lisbon has left us both enchanted and eager for tomorrow's adventures. lisbon, with its rich culture and exquisite culinary experiences, continues to captivate us.

save this for your next trip to lisbon, portugal 🇵🇹🚃 portugal lisbon thingstodo travel europe  discover 8 essential things to do in lisbon! from pink street to iconic pastries, get ready for an unforgettable trip. portugal lisbon thingstodo travel europe lisbon, portugal's picturesque capital, is filled with vibrant culture, stunning architecture, and delectable cuisine. whether you're a first-time visitor or returning to explore its hidden gems, here are eight must-do activities for an unforgettable experience in this charming city.  1. **stroll down pink street**: a popular nightlife hub, pink street is known for its lively atmosphere and vibrant bars. perfect for enjoying a pre-dinner drink while soaking in the city's energy.  2. **ride the elevador da bica**: this iconic funicular offers breathtaking views of the narrow streets below. a must-ride experience that combines stunning scenery with a taste of lisbon's historic charm.  3. **visit lx factory**: this former industrial site has transformed into a creative space filled with shops, restaurants, and art galleries. make sure to explore the unique boutiques and enjoy a meal at one of the trendy eateries.  4. **discover the padrāo dos descobrimentos**: this impressive monument celebrates the age of discoveries and provides insight into portugal’s maritime heritage. climb to the top for fantastic views of the tagus river.  5. **explore the green street**: this charming area is perfect for a relaxing stroll. enjoy the greenery and locally inspired artwork scattered around this peaceful section of the city.  6. **taste pastéis de belém (custard tart)**: no trip to lisbon is complete without indulging in a famous pastéis de nata from pastéis de belém. this creamy custard tart is a true portuguese delight!  7. **walk the beautiful streets**: lisbon’s streets are lined with beautiful tiles and captivating street art. take time to wander and capture the essence of the city’s stunning architecture.  8. **relax at empire square garden**: finally, unwind at this serene garden. enjoy the lush landscape and take a moment to appreciate the tranquil setting amidst the bustling city life.  these activities will immerse you in the heart of lisbon and leave you with lasting memories. prepare for an incredible adventure filled with culture, history, and delicious flavors!

🇵🇹 3 days in lisbon, this is how i’d do it (📌 save this for your next trip) day 1 🏡 start in alfama, just walk around and see where you end up ⛪ convento do carmo, one of those places that genuinely stops you 🚋 tram 28, yes it’s full of tourists, still worth it 🌅 miradouro de santa luzia for sunset, get there a bit early day 2 🏰 castelo de são jorge in the morning before it gets busy 🥐 pastéis de belém, queue, get two, eat them warm outside 🏛️ belém tower right after, it’s just down the road 🛥️ sunset boat on the tagus, honestly one of the best things i did day 3 ☕ café são miguel for coffee, skip the tourist spots 🎨 lx factory, especially on a sunday 📚 ler devagar, a bookstore inside an old factory, really cool 🍳 leonetta for brunch 🍸 icon bar rooftop to finish, drinks with the whole city in view a few things not to skip: a pastel de nata fresh and warm a sunset from a miradouro getting lost in alfama with nowhere to be 3 days goes fast. but lisbon stays with you 🇵🇹 follow @benihunyadi for more travel guides ✨ tag someone you’d do this with 🤍 lisbon lisbontravel portugaltravel travelblogger europetravel plan your perfect 3 days in lisbon with this itinerary covering alfama, belém tower, lx factory, and stunning viewpoints. save for your next trip! lisbon portugaltravel discover how to experience the vibrant city of lisbon in just three days with this curated itinerary, designed to help you make the most of your trip.  **day 1: historic charm and sunset views** begin your lisbon adventure by immersing yourself in the historic alfama district. allow yourself to wander through its narrow streets and discover hidden gems. visit the convento do carmo, a captivating ruin that is sure to leave a lasting impression. experience the iconic tram 28, a quintessential lisbon experience that, despite its popularity with tourists, remains a worthwhile journey. conclude your day with a breathtaking sunset at miradouro de santa luzia; arriving a bit early is recommended to secure a good spot.  **day 2: castles, pastries, and riverside serenity** start your second day at castelo de são jorge in the morning, before the crowds arrive. no trip to lisbon is complete without indulging in pastéis de belém; queue up, order two, and savor them warm outside. afterward, head to the nearby belém tower, a magnificent landmark. for a truly memorable experience, take a sunset boat cruise on the tagus river – it's considered one of the best activities in lisbon.  **day 3: local flavors and creative hubs** on your final day, start your morning with coffee at café são miguel, opting for local spots over tourist traps. explore lx factory, a trendy industrial complex, especially vibrant on sundays. inside, discover ler devagar, a unique bookstore housed within an old factory, offering a truly cool atmosphere. enjoy brunch at leonetta before finishing your day at icon bar rooftop for drinks with panoramic views of the entire city.  **unforgettable lisbon experiences:** don't miss out on savoring a fresh, warm pastel de nata. witnessing a sunset from a miradouro offers unparalleled beauty. getting intentionally lost in alfama, with no particular destination in mind, allows for authentic discovery. three days may pass quickly, but lisbon's charm will undoubtedly stay with you. follow @benihunyadi for more travel guides and tag someone you'd share this adventure with.

1. cais das colunas - step right to the edge of lisbon and feel the river vibes 🌊✨ perfect for golden hour pics and chilling by the water. 2. arco da rua augusta - famous landmark in the city center 🌟. walk under the arch, climb up for city views. 3. praça do comércio - iconic square ✨ huge open space, pastel buildings, river on one side. 4. jardim do torel - hidden garden vibes 🌿💛 tiny, peaceful, perfect to sit, relax, and take a break from the chaos.  5. tram 28 - hop on tram 28 and just goooo 🚋✨ you’ll pass through alfama and graça. epic photo ops literally at every turn. 6. miradouro porto de sol - it is stunning 🌅 watch alfama’s rooftops and the river glow in the sunset…it’s pure magic. 7. sé cathedral - it is breathtaking 😮 step inside and you can feel centuries of history around you. 8. miradouro da graça - it offers one of the best views in lisbon 🌇 perfect sunset spot. 9. street art - lisbon’s streets are full of art 🎨 from colorful murals to hidden graffiti. 10. pink street - once the red-light district, now lisbon’s nightlife hotspot full of bars and clubs. 11. money museum - check out the money museum 💰 explore portugal’s currency history and rare coins. it’s totally free. 12. jerónimos monastery - unesco world heritage site 🏛️ built in the 16th century in manueline style, located in belém. famous for its architecture and tombs of explorers. 13. 25 de abril bridge - lisbon’s iconic red suspension bridge 🌉  14. belém tower - historic fortress 🏰 built in the early 16th century to defend the city, unesco world heritage site, and full of history. 15. lx factory - old industrial complex turned creative hotspot 🎨 shops, bookstores, street art, and cool cafés…lisbon’s trendiest hangout. 16. time out market - food heaven in one place 🍴 from traditional portuguese dishes to creative bites, you’ll taste the city in every corner. 17. green street - lisbon’s streets are full of greenery 🌿 tree-lined avenues, less crowded than pink street.  18. the fantastic world of portuguese sardines - iconic lisbon souvenir shop 🐟 from decorative tins to sardine-themed gifts. this shop in lisbon is a  must-visit for a true taste of portuguese culture. 19. elevador de santa justa - iconic elevator in the heart of lisbon 🏗️ built in 1902, the top gives amazing city views…don’t miss it! 20. parque eduardo vii - lisbon’s biggest park 🌳 great for walks, city views, and taking a break from the hustle.  21. sintra- magical town just outside lisbon 🏰 hills, palaces, and forests…feels like stepping into a fairy tale.🧚 22. quinta da regaleira - enchanting estate in sintra 🌿 gothic architecture, secret tunnels, and mysterious gardens…explore and get lost in the magic. 23. national palace of sintra - historic palace in the heart of sintra 🏰 famous for its iconic twin chimneys and royal history…step inside to see centuries of portuguese heritage. traveldestinations lisbontravel thingstodoinlisbon lisbon europetravel

5 super fun workshops you can do in lisbon! 🎉   perfect for these, what feel like, never ending rainy days 😩🌧️✨. also perfect for valentine’s day activities 😉❤️ here are some amazing workshops to try: 1️⃣ pottery at disco wheel – not your typical pottery class! dim lights, drinks if you like, a pro demonstration, and then it’s your time to shine. make up to two pieces and pick them up in a few weeks. 🏺 2️⃣ song dynasty tea whisking with hannah – learn the art of matcha and powdered tea, whisk it to perfection, and paint on the foam with tea powder ink. finish with homemade chinese sweets (the mochi is a must!) 🍵✨ @hannahshappybelly  3️⃣ dine & draw with polly – guided exercises, drawing with your non-dominant hand, ridiculous scenes, and lots of laughs. polly hosts public and private events around the city. 🎨😂 @pom_lette  4️⃣ pasta making at bacco studio – fresh pasta from scratch, step-by-step guidance, unlimited wine, and a homemade pasta sauce to enjoy at the end. perfect for friends, birthdays, or a foodie date. 🍝🍷  5️⃣ cake decorating with carolina bakes – learn frosting and piping techniques, create your own beautiful cake, and take it home. a sweet experience you won’t forget. 🎂💛 @carolina  check out upcoming dates on their pages and tag a friend you’d like to do a workshop with or don’t, these are great to do solo too 🙂 also, if you have any workshop suggestions please let me know in the comments. i’m kind of addicted to them now 😬 lisbonactivities lisbonworkshops lisbonpottery thingstodoinlisbon lisbontips

portugal coffee rules nobody explains before your trip 🇵🇹 save this. send it to whoever you’re travelling with. ☕ bica — espresso. the lisbon word. say «uma bica», stand at the counter, drink it in two minutes. that’s how it works here. ☕ cimbalino — same drink, porto name. order this in lisbon and people will smile. order bica in porto — same thing. ☕ galão — tall glass, espresso and foamed milk, roughly 1:3. closest to a latte. sit-down drink, not a counter drink. ☕ meia de leite — half coffee, half hot milk, in a cup. softer than a bica. morning drink. ☕ pingado — espresso with one drop of milk. literally «dripped.» for when black is too much. ☕ abatanado — longer espresso, more water. weaker, but not an americano. how to order: «um café, por favor» — espresso «sem açúcar» — no sugar «com leite» — with milk tea? portugal is not a tea country. a bag in hot water — that’s it. every time. one more thing: coffee in portugal costs €0.80–1.20 at the counter. sit down at a table — price goes up. stand at the counter like a local — better coffee, better price, better experience. write me your travel dates to plan an unforgettable trip to portugal! coffeeportugal lisbontravel portugaltraveltips bicacoffee travelportugal learn portugal coffee names, how to order, and where to stand for the best price—master bica, cimbalino, galão and more. coffeeportugal lisbontravel portugaltraveltips portugal’s coffee culture is compact, specific and easy to learn—here’s a practical guide to ordering like a local. the basics: at the counter is the norm for quick, high-quality coffee at a low price; sitting at a table increases cost and changes the pace.  key coffee names and what they mean - bica — the lisbon word for a straight espresso. ask for “uma bica,” stand at the counter and drink it in two minutes. fast, strong and standard. - cimbalino — the same espresso in porto. using “cimbalino” in porto will feel natural; saying “bica” in porto or “cimbalino” in lisbon usually gets a friendly smile and the same drink. - galão — served in a tall glass, roughly one part espresso to three parts foamed milk. closest to a latte and meant as a sit-down drink rather than a quick counter espresso. - meia de leite — half coffee, half hot milk served in a cup. softer than a bica and commonly a morning choice. - pingado — an espresso with a single drop of milk. literally “dripped,” for when black coffee is too strong. - abatanado — a longer espresso with more water; weaker than a bica but distinct from an americano.  simple ordering phrases - “um café, por favor” — an espresso, please. - “sem açúcar” — without sugar. - “com leite” — with milk.  tea note portugal is not a tea country. tea typically comes as a bag in hot water—no elaborate tea culture to expect.  pricing and etiquette counter coffee generally costs between €0.80 and €1.20. if you sit at a table, expect the price to rise. to experience coffee like a local: stand at the counter, drink it quickly, enjoy the better price and the brisk, social rhythm of portuguese cafés.  closing and travel tip memorize one or two local words—“uma bica” in lisbon or “um cimbalino” in porto—and try standing at the counter once. small choices like these make everyday travel feel more authentic. want help planning coffee stops or a full portugal itinerary? share your travel dates and preferences to start building an unforgettable trip.  coffeeportugal lisbontravel portugaltraveltips

one of the coolest places to visit in lisbon! 📸✨🏭 📍 once an old industrial complex for textiles, the @lxfactory has transformed into a vibrant, artistic village nestled within the city. here, abandoned warehouses are now cafés, restaurants, concept stores and more! 🧺 you should visit on a sunday, when there is a weekly flea market with dozens of temporary booths and stalls, where you can find locally-made jewelry, vintage clothes, cork-crafted items, plants and more. 🎨 do not hesitate to go inside the big industrial building where you will find many boutiques, concept stores, art studios and creative company offices. 📚 lastly, check out the ler devagar bookstore! @lerdevagar, meaning “read slowly” is more than a bookstore; it is also a cultural space for events, temporary and permanent exhibitions. it was actually named one of the top ten most beautiful bookstores in the world! planning on visiting lisbon soon? save this reel and follow @thetravellingfrenchy for the best tips 🫶🏼   lxfactory lisbon lisbontravel lisbonportugal🇵🇹 lisbonguide     unique experiences in lisbon • things to do in lisbon • unconventional lisbon adventures • hidden gems in lisbon • unusual lisbon • off-the-beaten-path lisbon discover lx factory lisbon — transformed warehouses with cafés, boutiques, a sunday flea market, and the famous ler devagar bookstore. tips for visiting and what to see. lxfactory lisbon lisbontravel lead lx factory in lisbon is a repurposed industrial complex turned creative village — one of the city's coolest places to visit. once textile warehouses, the area now houses cafés, restaurants, concept stores, art studios, creative offices and a weekly flea market that draws locals and travelers alike.  what lx factory offers - transformed industrial buildings: large factory halls and loft-like spaces converted into shops, studios and eateries. the industrial architecture and street art create a distinctly urban, artistic atmosphere. - cafés and restaurants: a mix of casual cafés and inventive restaurants occupying former warehouse spaces, ideal for coffee, brunch or a relaxed meal between browsing stores. - concept stores and boutiques: independent shops selling locally-made jewelry, cork-crafted items, design objects and unique homeware. - art studios and creative offices: galleries, studios and small creative companies open to the public or visible from communal areas. - ler devagar bookstore: a standout cultural space named “read slowly.” more than a bookstore, it hosts events, exhibitions and was listed among the world’s most beautiful bookstores.  sunday flea market (best time to visit) - when to go: sundays are recommended for the weekly flea market. numerous temporary booths and stalls appear across the complex. - what to find: vintage clothes, locally-made jewelry, cork products, plants and other handmade or secondhand items — great for souvenirs and discovering local makers.  practical tips for visitors - timing: visit on sunday for the full market experience; weekdays are quieter but still great for cafés and shops. - explore inside big industrial buildings: many boutiques, concept stores and studios are housed inside the larger structures — don’t just stick to the outer courtyards. - plan for photos: the combination of industrial architecture and street art makes lx factory a photogenic spot — bring a camera. - combine with a lisbon itinerary: lx factory fits well into an afternoon exploring alternative lisbon neighborhoods and cultural sites.  why lx factory is worth it lx factory offers an unconventional lisbon experience that contrasts with the city’s historic districts. it’s a creative hub where local design, food and culture meet in reclaimed industrial spaces — perfect for travelers seeking hidden gems and unique experiences.  close / call to action planning a lisbon trip? save this guide and visit lx factory on a sunday to catch the flea market and ler devagar bookstore. follow @thetravellingfrenchy for more lisbon tips and unconventional experiences.

48 hours in lisbon. traveling this city with a toddler is no joke! here are the kid friendly activities we did: 1. stay in chiado/baixa area. completely flat area filled with shops and those lovely purple jacada trees. 2. take a tuktuk tour. it’s the easiest way to cover the whole city and all those hills. so helpful since we’re using a stroller. 3. have the tuktuk take you to all the scenic miradouros for the views! 4. try as many pastel de nadas as possible. our personal fave was at @manteigaria - pasteis de nata  5. check out the feira da ladra flea market in alfama district. open tuesday and fridays * not pictured but the time out market and aquarium were other favorites.  * also check out the 2nd largest @zara store! it was huge and i wanted everything!!! visitlisbon travelingwithkids lisbon toddlertravel travel  explore kid-friendly activities in lisbon with our toddler travel guide. discover the best spots and tips for family trips! visitlisbon travelingwithkids traveling with a toddler in lisbon can be quite an adventure! with its charming streets and rich culture, lisbon offers plenty of activities that the whole family can enjoy. here’s how to make the most of 48 hours in this beautiful city with your little one.  **stay in the chiado/baixa area**   choosing the right area to stay is crucial, especially with a toddler. the chiado and baixa districts are perfect for family travelers. these areas are flat, making it easier for strollers, and are filled with shops, cafes, and the stunning purple jacaranda trees that blossom in spring.  **take a tuktuk tour**   navigating lisbon’s hills can be challenging, but a tuktuk tour makes it simple. it’s a great way to cover a lot of ground in a short time. these little vehicles are stroller-friendly, and the drivers can take you to various attractions, minimizing the walking for your tired little ones.  **visit scenic miradouros**   ensure your tuktuk tour includes stops at the miradouros (viewpoints) around the city. the breathtaking vistas of lisbon are a must-see, and they offer perfect photo opportunities for those special family memories.  **savor local pastries**   no visit to lisbon is complete without trying pastel de nata. make it a mission to sample as many as possible! one of the best spots is @manteigaria, where you can enjoy this delicious custard pastry fresh out of the oven.  **explore feira da ladra flea market**   an exciting outing for the family can be found at the feira da ladra flea market, located in the alfama district. the market operates on tuesdays and fridays and offers a unique shopping experience. while it's lively and colorful, you can also find some great trinkets to remember your trip.  **other highlights**   although not pictured, two other spots that are highly recommended are the time out market for some delicious food options and the aquarium, which is sure to entertain the little ones. and if you're a fashion enthusiast, make sure to check out the second-largest @zara store while in the area!  in just 48 hours, you can create wonderful memories with your toddler in lisbon, filled with food, fun, and fantastic views. plan ahead, and enjoy your family adventure!

things to do in lisbon portugal.  i tried both the tram28 and the tuktuk tour in lisbon, and while tram 28 is the city's most famous ride, the tuk tuk gave me a completely different perspective. one is cheaper and iconic, the other is more comfortable, informative, and takes you places the tram can't. save this for your lisbon itinerary and send it to your travel partner @visitportugal  visitlisbonvisitlisbonavel lisbontips travelcreator traveltok  compare lisbon's tram 28 and tuk tuk tours to decide which fits your itinerary—cost, comfort, coverage, and highlights explained. visitlisbon visitlisbonavel lisbontips travelcreator traveltok lead lisbon visitors often face a choice between the city's iconic tram 28 and a tuk tuk tour. both offer sightseeing but deliver very different experiences. this guide compares cost, comfort, route access, and the type of perspective each tour provides so you can pick the best fit for your lisbon itinerary.  why choose tram 28 - iconic and historic: tram 28 is the city's most famous ride and a classic lisbon experience. riding it is a way to connect with the city's character and vintage transport culture. - cheaper option: generally more affordable than private tuk tuk tours, making it a good choice for budget-conscious travelers. - scenic route through neighborhoods: the tram passes many traditional quarters, giving a snapshot of lisbon's steep streets and architecture.  why choose a tuk tuk tour - more comfortable ride: tuk tuks offer a smoother, more comfortable experience than the crowded, rickety tram cars. - informative guides: tuk tuk tours are often led by drivers who provide live commentary, local tips, and context you might miss on the tram. - access to places the tram can't: tuk tuks can reach narrow streets, viewpoints, or off-the-beaten-path spots where the tram tracks don't go, offering a different perspective of lisbon.  how to decide for your itinerary - prioritize budget and tradition: choose tram 28 if you want the iconic, economical experience and are comfortable standing or squeezing into a busy car. - prioritize comfort and local insight: choose a tuk tuk if you want personalized commentary, better seats, and access to areas the tram misses. - mix both for a full view: take tram 28 for the classic ride and a tuk tuk later to explore hidden viewpoints and hear local stories.  practical tips - timing: tram 28 can be crowded during peak hours; try early morning or late afternoon. book a tuk tuk in advance for preferred time slots. - safety and belongings: on the tram, watch for pickpockets in crowded sections. in tuk tuks, secure loose items and follow driver instructions. - duration and routes: tram 28 follows a fixed historic route. tuk tuk tours vary in length and can be customized to include specific viewpoints or neighborhoods.  closing / call to action save this comparison for your lisbon itinerary and share it with your travel partner. whether you pick tram 28 for the iconic ride or a tuk tuk for comfort and local insight, both add memorable layers to exploring lisbon. which would you try first on your trip to lisbon?

✨ nearly free lisbon — 5 budget-friendly experiences you can’t miss 🇵🇹 ✨ lisbon is one of those cities where the best moments don’t come with a big price tag. if you’re traveling on a budget (or just want more pastéis and vinho in your life), these nearly free experiences deserve a spot on your itinerary👇 🚋 ride tram 28 one of the most iconic ways to see lisbon. this historic tram winds through alfama, graça, and baixa, climbing steep hills and passing postcard views the whole way. a single ride is ~€3.10 onboard, but it’s cheaper if you grab a viva viagem card and load it with rides or a day pass. pro tip: ride early morning or late evening to avoid crowds. 🌄 miradouro da graça just a short walk from the graça tram stop, this viewpoint is completely free and offers sweeping views over lisbon’s red rooftops and the tagus river. perfect for a rest stop, photos, or a sunset break. 🎶 free fado at tasca do chico experience traditional portuguese fado without a cover charge. there’s no minimum spend, but reservations (at least a day ahead) and cash are key. music usually starts around 9pm, and seats fill fast — this is one of the most authentic, low-cost nights out in lisbon. 🎨 explore lx factory set under the ponte 25 de abril bridge, lx factory is an old industrial complex turned creative hub. wandering is free, and you’ll find murals, indie shops, bookstores, and cafés — a great contrast to lisbon’s historic neighborhoods. 🏰 belém on a budget admire the torre de belém from the outside (free and just as impressive), then skip the entrance fee and spend those euros on a pastel de nata from the legendary pastéis de belém nearby. worth it every single time. 💡 why lisbon is perfect for budget travelers: walkable neighborhoods, affordable public transit, free viewpoints my mini second hand shopping crawl in lisbon  📍a outra face da lua curated and elevated. lots of accessories and vintage designer bags.  📍flamingo vintage kilo lots of fun to rummage. y2k & 90’s finds 📍humana you’ll find these charity shops in lots of european cities. this is were you’re going to find your €5 gem.  and what cities you’d like recommendations for next?  lisbonlisbontravelguidevintageclothingsecondhandthingstodoinlisbon  discover three curated vintage stores in central lisbon—a outra face da lua, flamingo vintage kilo, and humana—for designer bags, y2k finds, and €5 gems. lisbon vintageclothing secondhand lead three must-visit vintage stores in central lisbon sit just minutes apart and each offers a different secondhand treasure: curated accessories and designer bags at a outra face da lua; rummage-ready y2k and ’90s pieces at flamingo vintage kilo; and budget-friendly charity finds at humana where you can score a €5 gem.  why these three these shops are within a short walk of one another in the centre of lisbon, making them perfect for a compact shopping crawl. they represent three common secondhand shopping experiences: curated and elevated pieces, kilo-style digging for era-specific fashion, and charity-shop bargains.  what to expect - a outra face da lua: a curated store with elevated vintage pieces and lots of accessories and designer bags. ideal if you want quality finds and statement pieces.  - flamingo vintage kilo: a fun, rummage-style kilo shop with strong y2k and 1990s selections. great for browser shoppers who enjoy hunting through racks for unique items priced by weight.  - humana: a charity shop common across many european cities. expect low-priced treasures — this is the place to look for a genuine €5 find.  how to plan your crawl 1. start early to avoid crowds and get first pick.  2. wear comfortable shoes — all three stores are only minutes apart, but you’ll be standing and browsing a lot.  3. bring a reusable bag for purchases and small change for quick payments.  4. give each store at least 20–30 minutes: curated shops reward slower browsing; lisbontravel thingstodoinlisbon budgettraveltips lisbon is a stunning city filled with charm, culture, and history—and it doesn't have to break the bank! with various activities and experiences that cost less than 10€, budget travelers can explore the best of what lisbon has to offer without compromising on quality. here are 10 amazing things to do in lisbon that are wallet-friendly and will give you an authentic taste of the city.  1. **visit miradouros (viewpoints)**: lisbon is known for its breathtaking viewpoints, or 'miradouros', that offer stunning vistas of the city and the tagus river. places like miradouro da senhora do monte are free to visit, allowing you to snap incredible photos of the skyline.  2. **sample local bites at mercado da ribeira**: this iconic food market, also known as time out market, features a range of local delights. while some dishes can be pricey, you can find affordable bites to sample, keeping it under 10€.  3. **explore alfama’s narrow streets**: strolling through the historic alfama district is a must. the labyrinth of narrow streets is free to explore and filled with colorful buildings, street art, and a vibrant atmosphere.  4. **ride tram 28**: hop on the famous tram 28 for a scenic ride through the city’s hills. a single ticket is typically under 3€, making it a great budget option to see key landmarks.  5. **visit the centro cultural de belém**: offering a mix of exhibitions, concerts, and cultural events, many activities here are free or low-cost. check their calendar for free entrance days.  6. **take a walk along the riverfront**: lisbon's waterfront scenery is beautiful and accessible. enjoy a serene walk along the tagus river, taking in the sights of praça do comércio and the 25 de abril bridge.  7. **enjoy fado music**: while many fado venues can be costly, there are spots, particularly in alfama, where you can hear live fado music without spending too much. be sure to ask locals for tips!  8. **visit the national museum of ancient art**: this museum offers a free entrance on specific days of the month, making it an excellent opportunity to explore lisbon's art scene without spending much.  9. **discover jardim do príncipe real**: this picturesque garden is perfect for picnics and relaxation. enjoy nature and local markets often held in the area, all at little to no cost.  10. **relax at praia de carcavelos**: just a short train ride from the city, this beautiful beach is free to visit. spend a day soaking up the sun and enjoying the ocean waves—perfectly budget-friendly!  these activities are not only affordable but also offer a unique perspective of lisbon that many tourists might overlook. save this list for your next trip and don't forget to follow @martaplore for more budget travel guides!

lisbon portugal travel american tips for ordering in english in lisbon and using “obrigada” naturally. what to expect, polite phrases, and when to switch to portuguese. lisbon portugal travel american lead visitors in lisbon often order their food and drinks in english and then finish with the portuguese word “obrigada.” this short guide explains what to expect, how to use “obrigada” correctly, and simple language tips to stay polite while traveling in portugal.  body why people mix english and “obrigada” many travelers start interactions in english because it’s quick and widely understood in lisbon’s tourist areas. ending with “obrigada” (if you identify as female) or “obrigado” (if you identify as male) is a polite, friendly way to acknowledge local language and culture without needing full portuguese fluency.  when to use “obrigada/obrigado” - after receiving service (food, drink, or help).  - when a staff member goes out of their way or speaks to you in portuguese.  - as a quick, respectful closing to a transaction.  pronunciation and gender note - pronounce “obrigada” roughly as oh-bree-gah-dah and “obrigado” as oh-bree-gah-doh.  - use the form that matches your gender identity when speaking. locals will understand the intent either way, but matching form is conventional.  polite starter phrases to pair with english orders - “hello” or “hi” — most servers will respond in english if comfortable. - simple portuguese phrases that are easy to learn: “por favor” (please), “obrigado/obrigada” (thank you), and “com licença” (excuse me).  what to expect from locals - in central lisbon and tourist neighborhoods, staff commonly reply in english or a mix of english and portuguese. - using “obrigada/obrigado” is appreciated and seen as courteous; it rarely causes confusion.  quick tips for smoother interactions - if you want to try portuguese, keep phrases short and clear.  - speak slowly in english and avoid slang that may be unclear.  - smile and use body language—locals value politeness and effort.  close finishing an english order in lisbon with “obrigada” is a small, effective way to show respect for local language and culture. it’s simple to learn, widely appreciated, and helps make everyday interactions friendlier while you travel.  engagement prompt have you used “obrigada” or “obrigado” while traveling? share a quick experience or your favorite portuguese phrase to help other travelers.

living in lisbon, portugal  re-sharing from last month lisbon lisboa livingabroad dayinthelife portugal  experience a typical day of an american remote worker in lisbon, filled with running, art, and exploration. join the journey through this beautiful city! lisbon lisboa livingabroad dayinthelife portugal **a day in the life of an american living in lisbon, portugal** 🇵🇹  living in the beautiful city of lisbon is a unique experience, especially when you’re working remotely. here's a snapshot of a typical day in my life as an american expat enjoying all this vibrant city has to offer.   🌅 i kicked things off with an early morning run—though i must confess, the running footage i used was from a few days back. but hey, it's the same picturesque route! after my exercise, i returned home to prepare a delicious breakfast for my son and me. the view while we ate was simply stunning!  📚 once my son started his schooling, i settled down to tackle some work for a few hours. naturally, with all that focus, i felt i deserved a little reward. i headed over to my favorite coffee shop to indulge in some reading—perfectly cozy!  🥪 later, i had a few errands on my list. on our way, my son requested a sandwich, so we made a quick pit stop before i grabbed my second matcha of the day. it’s safe to say i have a bit of a matcha obsession!   🛍️ our travels took us to the mall where i needed to visit sephora for some winter face lotion for my son. i must mention—the selection there could use some work, but the view from the store? absolutely breathtaking!  🎨 next on the agenda was my art supply stop. reflecting on the past month, which brought some challenges and changes to my mental health, i turned to watercolor painting for solace. despite my inexperience, i couldn't resist picking up some quality art supplies—i guess i just have an eye for beautiful things!   ☀️ after dropping off my son at his extracurricular activity, i soaked up some sun while waiting. can you believe it was december and the temperature was a warm 70 degrees?   🏡 we decided to stroll home to enjoy the perfect weather, but not before making another matcha stop, of course! and what’s an outing without some delicious salted cream ice cream? it’s dangerously delightful!   📬 i had planned to get some work done when we got home, but the postal service (the ctt) threw a wrench in those plans. navigating their system can be quite the challenge—like the dmv back in the states! after a frustrating 45 minutes, i finally sorted things out and could unwind a bit.  🌇 as evening approached, i treated myself to some painting time while watching the beautiful sunset. after finishing up my work, i wrapped up the day by making dinner, feeling grateful for the experiences lisbon has brought my way.   every day is an adventure here in portugal!   lisbon livingabroad portugal dayinthelife americanexpat arttherapy coffeeculture remotework

great for azulejos (portuguese tiles), handmade goods, and fado-related souvenirs. - avenida da liberdade: lisbon’s luxury shopping avenue with high-end international labels and flagship stores. - lx factory: converted industrial complex hosting design shops, bookstores, and artisanal items—perfect for creative souvenirs and photography. - local markets: feira da ladra (flea market) for vintage and secondhand treasures; time your visit for market days.  what to buy - portuguese ceramics and azulejos: tiles, plates, decorative pieces reflecting local patterns. - cork products: bags, wallets, and eco-friendly accessories unique to portugal. - port wine and olive oil: packaged well for travel; verify customs rules for your destination. - local fashion and footwear: portuguese shoe brands and independent designers. - pastries and sweets: pastéis de nata are iconic—buy fresh or boxed as a treat for the journey. - handmade jewelry and artisanal crafts from lx factory and small boutiques.  planning your route and timing - start in baixa/chiado for central access, then walk or take short trams to bairro alto and alfama. - reserve lx factory for an afternoon visit when shops and cafés are open and the light is good for filming. - visit markets early for best finds and smaller crowds. - allow time for coffee breaks and pastries to capture lifestyle moments for the vlog.  practical shopping tips - carry a reusable bag and a small backpack for purchases. - check vat refund rules if you plan major purchases and are a non-eu resident. - wear comfortable shoes—cobblestone streets are common. - keep cash for small shops and markets; chiado lisbon portugal walk lisbon portugal lisboa chiado hindisong  discover the vibrant chiado district in lisbon, portugal. join us for a scenic walk showcasing this historic area! lisbon portugal lisboa chiado chiado is one of the most exciting neighborhoods in lisbon, portugal, captivating visitors with its blend of history, culture, and modern flair. a walk through chiado offers a unique glimpse into the vibrant life of this district, filled with charming cafes, historic boutiques, and stunning architecture. as you stroll through the cobblestone streets, you'll be surrounded by beautiful buildings that showcase a variety of architectural styles, from neoclassical to art nouveau.  one of the must-see spots in chiado is the lively praça luís de camões, a bustling square that serves as a meeting point for locals and tourists alike. here, you can sit back and enjoy a coffee from one of the many outdoor cafés or simply soak in the atmosphere. the square is also a great starting point for exploring the narrow streets that lead to hidden gems like bookstores, art galleries, and artisan shops.  chiado is renowned for its historical significance as well. it's the neighborhood where some of portugal's greatest writers and poets once gathered, including fernando pessoa. visit the iconic a brasileira café, which has become a cultural landmark, to see the bronze statue of pessoa and enjoy a traditional pastéis de nata.  the district vibrates with energy and offers a perfect blend of old and new, making it an essential stop for anyone traveling to lisbon. whether you’re enjoying the vibrant shopping scene or exploring the historical sites, a walk through chiado promises memorable experiences and beautiful sights. don't forget to capture the picturesque moments and share them on your journey! if you're planning a trip to lisbon or exploring its cultural heart, be sure to include chiado in your itinerary for an unforgettable experience.

a few things to highlight :  transport : we caught ubers & tram, ubers are super cheap in lisbon so we preferred doing that but also some places are very walkable  restaurants : make sure to book any before travelling as some are booked out. we had to wait 1.5 hours for ponte final.  cafes : most are walk ins  activities: make sure to book any palaces in sintra in advance as it gets busy.  day 1 : explore chiado, shopping street & the plaza. as well as cafe hopping & trying many pastel de natas. i recommend grabbing brunch at heim, rhodo, dear breakfast. also check out the tram 28 area to see the famous tram.  day 2 : we decided on doing our day trip on the 2nd day, we took an uber from etma bakery to quinta de regaleria, you can catch the train however the uber was cheap so we decided to save time by doing so. we only spent 2 hours in quinta however i would’ve stayed longer had we not had timed tickets to pena. so definitely consider spending longer between the both. it was also another 20 min uber to get to pena. then we went to pena & spent a couple of hours exploring, pena was abit underwhelming in comparison to quinta. after that we headed back to lisbon & checked out the time out markets whilst waiting for our res at lupita. this place is ranked top 20 in europe & it did not disappoint. there was a 1.5 hour wait for it though.  day 3 : we spent the day exploring alfama, the cathedral and saturday markets. brunch at rhodo which was amazing, after we finished exploring we headed to maat museum and checked it out. then went back and strolled around the city before heading to zubir chicken for one last feed. the best chicken ever.  enjoy!  lisbon lisbonfoodguide travellisboa travelvlog lisbonthingstodo

a slower way of travel in sintra sintra penapalace quintadaregaleira itinerary portugaltravel  mapped 4 days lisbon itinerary how to spend a perfect day in sintra what to do in sintra plan the perfect sintra day trip from lisbon: choose pena palace or quinta da regaleira, lunch in town, try a travesseiro, and return to lisbon. sintra penapalace quintadaregaleira lead spend day two of a four-day lisbon itinerary exploring sintra — a slower, charming escape that's too special to skip. pick one major attraction in the morning, rest and eat in town, then return to lisbon for a relaxed evening.  morning: choose one highlight - pena palace: the iconic, whimsical, romantic landmark. grounds are large and involve a lot of uphill walking. it draws crowds; prioritize booking the earliest time-slot tickets two to three weeks in advance to avoid lines and make the most of your morning. - quinta da regaleira: darker, more gothic fairy-tale atmosphere. famous for the initiation well but filled with many landscape features to discover across its gardens. equally large and requiring time to explore.  why choose one, not both both sites are extensive and involve more uphill walking than expected. trying to do both in one day often means rushing through each site and exhausting yourself. choose one to explore properly in the morning.  lunch and afternoon in town after your chosen palace or estate, head into sintra town for a later lunch and a slower pace. explore streets, visit a terrace for a wine break, or — if you're quick — pop into the national palace of sintra for a brief visit.  local treat to try stop at casa piriquita for a travesseiro, the local pastry invented there. options include the classic travesseiro and a nutella variation — both worth sampling.  return to lisbon return to lisbon in time for a simple dinner and to rest for day three of your itinerary.  takeaway for a stress-free, memorable sintra day trip: pick either pena palace or quinta da regaleira in the morning, book pena palace early if chosen, enjoy lunch and pastries in town, and save energy for the return to lisbon. questions: which palace would you visit first — pena palace or quinta da regaleira?

what i did in lisbon portugal for 2 days  lisbontravel portugal lisbonportugal cr7 europe travelportuguese discover my exciting 2-day journey through lisbon, portugal, filled with breathtaking sights and cultural experiences. lisbontravel portugal cr7 europe travel lisbon, the stunning capital of portugal, offers a rich tapestry of history, culture, and breathtaking sights that can be explored even in just 2 days. this article outlines an exciting itinerary for making the most of your short visit to this beautiful city.  on the first day, start your adventure in the historic district of alfama, one of lisbon's oldest neighborhoods. here, you can wander through narrow cobblestone streets, taking in the charming architecture and vibrant azulejos (ceramic tiles). make sure to stop by the iconic lisbon cathedral, a magnificent structure that showcases the city's medieval past.  lunch can be enjoyed at a local tasca, where you can savor delicious portuguese dishes such as bacalhau (codfish) or caldo verde (green soup). afterward, visit the são jorge castle, perched atop a hill, offering panoramic views of the city and the tagus river—a perfect spot for photography.  in the afternoon, head to the belém district, known for its rich maritime history. here, you can visit the belém tower and the jerónimos monastery, both unesco world heritage sites. don’t forget to indulge in a pastéis de nata (custard tart) at pastéis de belém, a must-try treat!  on the second day, consider exploring the vibrant neighborhood of bairro alto. known for its lively atmosphere, it's the perfect place to immerse yourself in lisbon's nightlife or discover unique cafes and shops during the day. additionally, take some time to explore the lx factory, a creative hub featuring street art, boutiques, and eateries, set in a transformed industrial space.  wrap up your lisbon adventure with a relaxing evening at a rooftop bar, admiring the sunset over the cityscape. with its friendly locals, diverse culinary scene, and picturesque landscapes, lisbon truly offers a memorable experience that attracts visitors from all over the world. whether you're a first-time traveler or returning to rediscover its charm, this city will undoubtedly leave a lasting imprint on your heart.

🇨🇱🇵🇹 más historias de chilenos residentes en lisboa: de la policía a arsmate.  ella es @victoria monsalve 🌸 (victoria_monss en instagram), ex carabinera que actualmente se dedica a la venta de contenido para adultos. la joven de 29 años vive en lisboa y acompañó a la roja en el amistoso ante portugal.  seleccionchilena laroja lisboa arsmate  historia de victoria monsalve, ex carabinera chilena que vive en lisboa y hoy vende contenido para adultos; acompañó a la roja en el amistoso ante portugal. seleccionchilena laroja lisboa arsmate victoria monsalve: ex carabinera chilena radicada en lisboa  victoria monsalve, de 29 años, es una joven chilena que dejó el servicio en la institución policial para iniciar una nueva etapa de vida en lisboa. actualmente se dedica a la venta de contenido para adultos y forma parte de la comunidad de chilenos residentes en la capital portuguesa.  trayectoria y cambio profesional  como ex carabinera, victoria proviene de una formación en la fuerza pública chilena. tras mudarse a lisboa, reconvirtió su carrera profesional y optó por monetizar contenido en plataformas de suscripción (arsmate figura como referencia en las etiquetas relacionadas). este cambio ilustra una transición personal y laboral de la seguridad pública a la industria del contenido adulto.  vida en lisboa y vínculo con el fútbol  la joven de 29 años vive en lisboa y fue acompañante de la selección chilena —la roja— durante el amistoso contra portugal, una experiencia que la conecta con la comunidad chilena local y con eventos deportivos internacionales.  contexto y etiquetas relevantes  la historia de victoria aparece en el marco de relatos sobre chilenos residentes en lisboa: desde profesionales que trabajan en seguridad hasta creadores de contenido. las etiquetas asociadas —seleccionchilena, laroja, lisboa, arsmate— contextualizan su perfil público y su presencia en redes.  ¿quieres saber más? lisbon portugal solotraveler traveltips traveling to lisbon, portugal can be an exciting adventure filled with vibrant culture, delicious cuisine, and stunning sights. based on my recent experience in this beautiful city, i have compiled four essential tips to make your visit enjoyable and memorable.  first and foremost, the lisbon airport experience can be quite chaotic, especially when leaving. it's crucial to arrive at the airport a minimum of three hours before your flight. i made the mistake of arriving only two hours ahead, thinking my tsa precheck would save me time, but i barely made it to boarding. so, take my advice: don’t risk it!  next, consider skipping some of the more touristy spots like the alex factory, pink street, and the time out market. while these locations are popular, they can sometimes feel overcrowded and less genuine. instead, check out the lisbon flea market on saturdays or go bar hopping in the vibrant barrio alto district. these experiences will give you a better taste of local life and culture.  another invaluable tip is to extend your stay to explore other cities. although i loved lisbon, i regretted not visiting porto during my time there. plan enough time to venture outside of lisbon for a day trip or more to experience the diverse beauty of portugal.  lastly, be mindful of your footwear. while the hills of lisbon may not seem treacherous, the cobblestone streets can be slippery. i witnessed someone fall while walking, so leave the heels at home. opt for comfortable shoes with good grip instead—your feet will thank you.  in addition to these tips, immerse yourself in the local culture by participating in tours, such as the african lisbon tour or afro flavors tour. these experiences help you engage with the rich history and diverse communities within the city.  don't forget to check out all my lisbon content on my page for more insights and feel free to reach out with any questions you may have! these are often served with a small packet of sugar or a sugar bowl. - variations: from plain sweetened espressos to meia de leite (coffee with milk) with sugar, sweetness levels vary by personal taste. how to order and taste like a local - ask for a bica for an espresso; specify “com açúcar” (with sugar) or “sem açúcar” (without sugar) if you have a preference. - if you prefer less sweetness, request “pouco açúcar” (little sugar). - try both ways: taste a plain espresso first, then add sugar to compare how it changes flavor. practical tips for visitors in lisbon and across portugal - expect sugar to be available at cafes; languages spoken in morocco. 🇲🇦  marrakech tarik_belasri tourguide morocco guidedtour  explore the fascinating languages of morocco and their impact on culture. discover the richness of moroccan linguistic diversity! marrakech tarik_belasri tourguide morocco guidedtour morocco is a country renowned for its rich cultural heritage and linguistic diversity. the primary languages spoken in morocco are arabic and berber, with french also widely used in various sectors, including education, business, and government.   the arabic dialect spoken in morocco, known as moroccan arabic or darija, is distinct from modern standard arabic, which is used in formal communication. darija incorporates elements from amazigh, spanish, and french, reflecting the country’s historical influences.   berber, recognized as an official language of the country, includes several dialects, such as tamazight and tachelhit. these languages carry deep cultural significance and express the identity of the berber people, who are indigenous to north africa.   french has a significant presence in urban areas and is often associated with the educational system and higher education institutions. its use showcases morocco's historical connection with france and facilitates communication in various professional fields.  in addition to these major languages, you can encounter various regional dialects and languages, such as spanish in the northern regions, reflecting morocco's diverse historical ties.    understanding the languages of morocco enriches your experience as a traveler. it allows you to connect deeper with the local culture and communities, enhancing your guided tours and interactions. engaging with locals in their native languages can lead to more meaningful conversations and a greater appreciation of morocco's vibrant identity.   discovering the linguistic diversity is an essential part of any visit to morocco, where each language tells a story and plays a role in the rich tapestry of moroccan culture. consider joining guided tours that highlight not only the sights but also the sounds and stories of this linguistically rich nation.

🇲🇦 the most beautiful riad in marrakech ⬇️  ⁣ ⁣ 🇲🇦➡️ get my free marrakech travel guide at marrakech.kelseyinlondon.com (link in bio) which includes:📍places to visit ⭐️ top things to do & activities 🍽️ best restaurants 🏩 hotel & riad recommendations 📆 best time to visit 🚕 how to get around⁣⁣ +  a saveable google map with all locations pinned 🗺️⁣ ⁣ recently awarded a michelin key, izza is one of the most unique places i've stayed in marrakech. hidden within the medina, this boutique hotel describes itself as a "house of friends" – a collection of interconnected historic homes where art, design, culture and hospitality come together.⁣ ⁣ from the moment i arrived, it felt less like checking into a hotel and more like stepping inside a beautifully curated art gallery. contemporary artworks fill the courtyards, communal spaces and guest rooms, with more than 300 pieces on display throughout the property.⁣ ⁣ the riad has just 14 individually designed rooms spread across three peaceful courtyards, with no two alike. i stayed in "jack", named after jack kerouac, which felt cosy yet surprisingly spacious thanks to clever design details, built-in wall niches and plenty of hidden storage.⁣ ⁣ one of my favourite things about izza was simply wandering around. each courtyard has its own personality, while the main heated pool stretches an impressive 10 metres long, large enough for proper laps, which is incredibly rare for a riad. there's also a smaller plunge pool, perfect after exploring the medina.⁣ ⁣ breakfast, served on the rooftop and included in the room rate, was another highlight. rather than a buffet, you'll find beautifully presented à la carte dishes, and i was particularly happy to discover they served iced matcha, something surprisingly difficult to scheduled coaches usually list trip times around 3.5–4 hours, factoring brief stops. premium or express coach services may be quicker.   - private car or taxi: direct private transfers on the highway can complete in about 3–3.5 hours if non-stop. travel time varies with driving style and traffic.   - self-drive: allow roughly the same time as private transfers; souks marrakech travelling exploring culture  explore the souks in marrakech: what to see, shop for, and how to navigate the medina. practical tips for first-time visitors. souks marrakech travelling exploring culture lead come to the souks in marrakech with us 🧡✨ — a walk through the winding markets of the medina where color, scent, and craft meet. this guide explains what to see in marrakech souks, what to buy, and simple ways to get the most from your visit.  what the souks are the souks are marrakech’s historic market quarter inside the medina. expect narrow alleys lined with stalls selling spices, leather goods, metal lamps, textiles, carpets, ceramics, and traditional crafts. the atmosphere is sensory: bright colors, crafted objects, and the scents of spices and leather.  highlights to see - spice and food alleys: jars and pyramids of saffron, cumin, ras el hanout, and colorful dried peppers. great for photos and buying small spice gifts. - leather and tanneries: handmade leather bags, slippers (babouches), and belts. look for quality stitching and natural dyes. - lamps and metalwork: brass and copper lamps, teapots, and mirrors with intricate patterns. - textiles and carpets: woven blankets, kilims, and embroidered fabrics. inspect weave and fringe to judge handmade quality. - ceramics and pottery: painted plates, tagines, and decorative tiles with geometric designs. - artisan workshops: watch craftsmen at work—carpet weaving, metal hammering, or leather finishing.  what to buy - small, easy-to-carry items: spices, small ceramics, leather accessories, scarves, and jewelry. these make good souvenirs and are simpler to pack. - larger purchases: rugs and furniture are available but check measurements, shipping options, and authenticity before buying.  practical tips for visitors - bargaining: haggling is normal. start lower than the first price and negotiate politely until you reach a fair middle ground. - cash: many stalls prefer cash (moroccan dirham). carry small notes and coins for small purchases. - navigation: alleys can be labyrinthine. note landmarks, save a map screenshot, or use a local guide to avoid getting lost. - timing: mornings are generally less crowded; late afternoon can be busier. some workshops close for a midday break. - respect: ask before photographing people or workshops, and be polite when handling goods.  safety and cultural notes - keep belongings secure and be mindful of pickpockets in crowded lanes. - dress respectfully in public spaces to align with local customs.  closing / takeaway the souks in marrakech offer a vivid, hands-on experience of moroccan craft and culture. plan what you want to see, carry cash, bargain kindly, and allow time to wander. ready to explore the alleys, scents, and colors? which stall would you visit first — spices, leather, or lamps?

henna art café et dar essalam — conseils et lieux à voir. marrakech jardinmajorelle jour 6 — marrakech : jardin majorelle, musée yves saint laurent, henna art café et dar essalam  aperçu la journée s’articule autour de quatre étapes proches et complémentaires : le jardin majorelle pour ses couleurs et sa collection botanique, le musée yves saint laurent pour la mode et l’histoire, le henna art café pour une pause conviviale autour de l’art du henné, puis le dar essalam pour l’atmosphère traditionnelle.  jardin majorelle le jardin majorelle est connu pour ses bassins, ses variétés végétales exotiques et le bleu majorelle omniprésent. prévoyez un créneau tôt le matin ou en fin d’après-midi pour éviter la foule. les points forts : les allées ombragées, les cactus et palmiers, les petites scènes aquatiques et les points photo autour du pavillon bleu.  musée yves saint laurent juste à côté, le musée yves saint laurent retrace le travail du couturier et présente des expositions temporaires autour de la mode. vérifier les horaires d’ouverture et réserver les billets en ligne si possible pour gagner du temps. à l’intérieur, repérer les archives, les silhouettes emblématiques et les expositions dédiées aux collaborations artistiques.  henna art café pour une pause atypique, le henna art café propose une ambiance créative dédiée au henné et à la détente. idéal pour goûter des spécialités locales, observer des réalisations au henné ou acheter des souvenirs artisanaux. c’est un bon endroit pour se reposer entre visites et vivre une expérience culturelle plus intime.  dar essalam clôturez la journée au dar essalam, lieu qui évoque l’architecture et l’hospitalité traditionnelles. que ce soit pour admirer la décoration, dîner ou simplement flâner dans ses espaces, dar essalam offre une atmosphère plus calme et authentique après les sites touristiques.  conseils pratiques - horaires : commencer tôt au jardin majorelle pour éviter la foule et la chaleur. - réservations : réserver le musée ysl à l’avance si possible. - transport : les sites sont proches mais prévoir taxi ou marche selon votre hébergement. - photographie : respecter les règles locales et les zones interdites aux photos. - budget : billets d’entrée pour le jardin et le musée, prévoir liquide pour achats au café et souvenirs.  conclusion cette journée à marrakech combine nature, art, mode et traditions : jardin majorelle pour les paysages, musée yves saint laurent pour la mode, henna art café pour une pause artisanale et dar essalam pour le charme traditionnel. idéal pour une journée riche en découvertes et en photos.  question pour les lecteurs quel lieu visiteriez-vous en premier : le jardin majorelle ou le musée yves saint laurent ?

haul incoming x marrakech  see a marrakech shopping haul with top souk finds, tips on what to buy, and must-see stalls to visit. marrakech lead a marrakech shopping haul captures colorful souk discoveries and the thrill of browsing winding stalls. this haul highlights the kinds of items shoppers bring home from marrakech, the atmosphere that makes pulling yourself out of the souks hard, and quick pointers for getting the most from a market visit.  body what shoppers typically find - textiles: handwoven rugs, embroidered cushions, and patterned scarves—lightweight pieces pack well and showcase moroccan patterns.  - ceramics: painted bowls, tagines, and decorative plates in vibrant blues and ochres. choose uncracked pieces and ask about packing for travel.  - leather goods: babouches (leather slippers), belts, and bags made in classic moroccan styles. inspect stitching and leather quality.  - metalwork and lanterns: brass and copper lanterns, trays, and tea sets with engraved details that add moroccan charm to any home.  - jewelry and accessories: silver or brass necklaces, rings, and beadwork reflecting local designs.  - spices and foodstuffs: saffron, ras el hanout, preserved lemons, and argan oil—buy sealed packaging for transport.  souk atmosphere and why it’s hard to leave the souks are a maze of narrow alleys, each dedicated to different crafts. the sensory mix—colors, textures, calls from vendors, and the smell of spices—makes lingering natural. sticker-worthy moments include finding a perfect rug or bargaining down to a fair price; these are the moments shoppers describe as “you’ll have to drag us out of these souks.”  practical shopping tips - bring cash: many stalls prefer dirham cash; small bills help.  - bargain respectfully: start lower than your maximum and expect friendly back-and-forth.  - check quality: look for even weaving on textiles, solid seams on leather, and undamaged ceramics.  - pack smart: request bubble wrap or ask your riad/hotel for packing tips. for fragile ceramics, consider shipping options if available.  - time your visit: mornings are quieter; late afternoon can be busier and more atmospheric.  - keep receipts for larger purchases and confirm export rules for antiques.  what this haul shows a typical marrakech haul mixes decorative home pieces, wearable finds, and culinary souvenirs. the haul demonstrates variety—textiles and ceramics often dominate footage and photos—while also showing how shoppers accumulate small treasures across multiple stalls.  close / call-to-action if planning a marrakech market trip, prioritize what you want to bring home—textiles and ceramics are the most iconic—and allow time to wander. which souk find would you want in your home: a rug, a lantern, or a tagine? share which item you’d hunt for on your marrakech haul.

the staff at fragrantica agreed we could film. wear your new scent around the medina to test longevity and public reaction—locals may stop to compliment or inquire, which happened to us. choose notes that suit your skin and climate; brighter citrus or spice-forward accords work well in hot weather, while deeper bases add staying power.  takeaway: a marrakech perfume workshop is both a souvenir and a personalized product that can spark real conversations. if you visit fragrantica or a similar artisan perfumer, expect guided blending, local ingredients, and the chance to leave with a bespoke scent that turns heads. would you try making your own perfume on a trip? share what notes you’d choose.

spend a day with me in marrakech🥰 vlog travelvlog capcut  join me for a day exploring marrakech! from a guided medina tour and the secret garden to finding the perfect rug and enjoying delicious food, experience the magic of morocco. spend a day with me in marrakech, morocco. this vlog captures our first day, starting with an early wake-up call for a guided tour of the medina. this allowed us to simply immerse ourselves in the experience without worrying about navigation.  it was incredibly fascinating to finally be in marrakech in person, especially after seeing so many videos on tiktok. the experience felt like stepping into a movie. we visited popular spots, including the secret garden, where i marveled at the exotic plants. one particular tree caught my eye; it looked like something out of a cartoon. while the garden was a bit crowded, it was still a beautiful and serene place with many butterflies.  we also stopped at various shops. although we tried on some items, nothing quite fit our style, and we weren't looking to buy just for the sake of it. however, the craftsmanship of marrakech was truly impressive to witness firsthand.  a highlight of the day was visiting a rug shop. after initially being hesitant, my friend was amazed by one rug, and then a stunning pink, double-sided rug was presented to me, which i absolutely had to have. it's destined for my walk-in closet. our sales associate, omar, even requested to be included in the vlog, so here he is!  next, we visited a store where i indulged in some perfume oil. i purchased a "sexy oil" and received some complimentary items, including moroccan lip balm products that i've seen all over tiktok.  following our shopping, we headed to lunch at a place that seemed to be called la jardine. honestly, it wasn't my favorite dining experience.  after a long day of walking, we returned to our hotel, which offered afternoon tea daily – a must for me! things got physical in marrakech! 🇲🇦 watch out for "fake guides" in the medina! this scammer wouldn't leave me alone.. scammer marrakech morocco travelsafety solotravel  spot fake guides in marrakech’s medina—learn signs, stay safe, and avoid persistent scammers while solo traveling. scammer marrakech morocco travelsafety solotravel things got physical in marrakech’s medina—a reminder to watch for “fake guides” who target visitors. in the crowded alleys of the medina, one scammer followed persistently and refused to leave, turning a routine walk into an uncomfortable encounter.  what happened the incident began like many tourist approaches: someone offering guidance and insisting on leading the way. instead of a friendly tip, the person became persistent and escalated to physical proximity that felt threatening. the traveler had to assert boundaries and leave the situation quickly.  how to spot fake guides - immediate insistence: they push to lead right away without you asking. genuine guides wait for consent.   - overfamiliarity: persistent touching, hovering, or refusing to give space.   - no official id: legitimate guides in morocco often carry id from recognized associations.   - demand for cash or sudden detours: they try to take you to shops or demand payment unexpectedly.   - pressure tactics: they use aggressive language or physical presence to coerce you.  practical safety steps - say no firmly and walk away toward a busy, open spot.   - seek help from nearby vendors, police or official tourist centers if the person won’t leave.   - carry a local sim or map app to show you don’t need guidance.   - travel with a visible confidence—stand straight, keep purposeful pace.   - pre-book official tours or hire guides from reputable agencies with credentials.  if things turn physical - prioritize getting to safety over confrontation. move to crowded areas or businesses.   - if you’re injured or feel endangered, contact local police and seek medical help if needed.   - report the incident to your accommodation so they can warn other guests.  final takeaway the medina in marrakech is vibrant and welcoming, but scams and aggressive fake guides can spoil the experience. recognize the red flags—insistence, lack of id, pressure for money or detours—and use clear refusal, distance, and official services to stay safe. have questions about navigating local guide options or want tips for solo travel safety in morocco? share your concerns and experiences to help others travel smarter.  scammer marrakech morocco travelsafety solotravel

from mandala society to sweet & souk, explore the city's culinary delights. marrakechfood marrakechfoodguide marrakech is a vibrant city known for its rich culture and exquisite food scene. if you're planning a food journey in this moroccan gem, then look no further! this guide will introduce you to the perfect day of food in marrakech, featuring some of the most delightful spots to tantalize your taste buds.  start your day at **mandala society**, a well-loved cafe known for its exquisite brunch. their dishes are not only delicious but also beautifully presented, making it the perfect place to kick off your food adventure. here, you can enjoy hearty breakfast options infused with local flavors, all while soaking up the atmosphere of this charming venue.  next, make your way to **foundouk gargaa**, a restaurant that combines traditional moroccan meals with a delightful twist. the ambiance and historic surroundings will enhance your dining experience as you sample a range of dishes. don't miss the chance to enjoy a tagine or a selection of moroccan appetizers here.  for lunch, head to **sandwich chez hicham**. this casual spot is famous for its scrumptious sandwiches bursting with flavor. ideal for a quick bite, it’s loved by locals and tourists alike. try their specialties for a satisfying and budget-friendly meal.  as the afternoon sun shines, treat yourself to a sweet stop at **sweet & souk**. this dessert haven offers a variety of mouth-watering sweets and pastries, making it a must-visit. perfectly located for an afternoon indulgence, their menu will surely satisfy your sweet tooth.  finally, gather the day's experiences at **bien venu**, where contemporary moroccan cuisine takes center stage. here, you can wind down with a delicious dinner, reflecting on your food journey through the city.  marrakech is not just a city of sights; it is a culinary destination that offers an array of flavors waiting to be explored. whether you’re a foodie or just looking to enjoy local dishes, these spots are sure to provide an unforgettable gastronomic experience. so pack your bags, take your appetite, and embark on this delicious food itinerary in marrakech!

i think anyone could spend the whole day in the souks 🇲🇦  moroccotravel marrakech marrakechtravel visitmorocco souks  marrakesh is a beautiful city that has a significant amount of tourists, bringing in around 4 million visitors yearly. filled with so much culture and food, we had a fun time exploring :)  a full day wandering marrakesh souks, tasting local food and soaking up culture—souks shopping, chez hicham break, jemaa el-fna sunset. moroccotravel marrakech souks marrakesh offers a day packed with colors, smells and local rhythms—perfect for anyone who could spend the whole day in the souks. start mid-morning: arrive at the souks around 9:36am and allow plenty of time to browse. the winding lanes reveal textiles, spices, lamps and handcrafted goods; plan to shop for a while and enjoy aimless wandering rather than a rushed checklist.  around mid-afternoon, take a food break at chez hicham (noted here at 3:00pm). a pause for local dishes refreshes you for more exploring and provides a true taste of marrakesh cuisine. after lunch, a quieter rest fits well—this itinerary mentions a hostel break at 5:02pm—use that time to recharge before the evening becomes lively.  return to the city as the light softens. by 8:35pm, head to jemaa el-fna for sunset and the changing atmosphere: street performers, food stalls and vibrant crowds. later in the night the juice stalls are irresistible (noted at 10:12pm), offering fresh flavors to close the evening. finish the day with rest—sleep is listed at 11:32pm—after a full immersion in culture and food.  practical tips: wear comfortable shoes for the souks, carry small change for purchases, and pace your day to include rest between busy market hours and the evening bustle. this realistic schedule captures a typical marrakesh day—market mornings, a satisfying lunch at a local spot like chez hicham, an afternoon rest, and an unforgettable evening at jemaa el-fna.

if you're visiting marrakech in the summer, jardin majorelle is the escape you need. 🌴💙 after days of wandering the bustling medina, stepping into these lush gardens felt like entering another world. the shade, the vibrant cobalt blue, the exotic plants, and the peaceful atmosphere made it one of the coolest—and most beautiful—experiences of our trip. your ticket can also include entry to the yves saint laurent museum, where you can explore the legendary designer's connection to marrakech and admire exhibits celebrating his iconic work. together, the gardens and museum make for the perfect half-day experience and are absolutely worth adding to your itinerary. pro tip: go early in the morning to enjoy cooler temperatures and fewer crowds. jardinmajorelle marrakechguide visitmorocco moroccotravel traveltiktok plan a half-day at jardin majorelle: cool shade, cobalt-blue gardens, exotic plants and ysl museum details. go early to avoid heat and crowds. jardinmajorelle marrakechguide visitmorocco why jardin majorelle is a summer must-see in marrakech  jardin majorelle offers a cool, colorful escape from marrakech’s summer heat. after navigating the busy medina, the garden’s shade, vibrant cobalt-blue structures, exotic plantings, and tranquil paths create a distinct, peaceful atmosphere—making it one of the most beautiful and refreshing stops on a morocco itinerary.  what to expect  - atmosphere: lush greenery, shaded walkways, water features, and the signature cobalt blue that defines the garden’s architecture. - highlights: exotic plants from around the world, quiet corners for photos, and thoughtfully designed vistas that contrast with the medina’s bustle. - combined visit: many tickets include entry to the yves saint laurent (ysl) museum, which explores the designer’s connection to marrakech and displays exhibits celebrating his work.  practical tips  - best time to go: early morning. cooler temperatures and far fewer crowds make the experience more comfortable and photogenic. - how long to plan: a half-day is ideal to see the garden at leisure and visit the ysl museum if included in your ticket. - tickets: check whether your ticket includes the ysl museum to avoid separate lines or extra fees.  why add it to your itinerary  jardin majorelle and the ysl museum together deliver a peaceful, culturally rich half-day that contrasts with the hectic market streets. the garden’s shade and cool microclimate are especially welcome during summer, and the visual impact of the cobalt-blue structures makes it a memorable highlight.  final takeaway  include jardin majorelle in your marrakech plans for a refreshing, beautiful experience—arrive early, allow a half-day, and consider a combined ticket for the ysl museum to complete the visit. jardinmajorelle marrakechguide visitmorocco
