how i spent a day in reykjavik, iceland without blowing my budget!  discover how to explore reykjavik on a budget with highlights and costs for each activity. enjoy your layover without overspending! reykjavik travel reykjavik, the capital of iceland, offers a plethora of attraction opportunities, even if you're on a budget. with careful planning, you can experience this vibrant city without breaking the bank. here’s how to make the most of a 22-hour layover in reykjavik without overspending.  start your journey with the essentials. shuttle buses to downtown reykjavik are approximately €30 each way, so plan your transportation wisely. once in the city, you have numerous free attractions to explore. visit the iconic hallgrimskirkja church, where entry is free. this stunning piece of architecture provides a great photo opportunity and is a must-see.  for those who enjoy a bit of shopping, take a stroll through downtown and engage in window shopping. this is a fantastic way to experience local culture without spending a cent. if your taste buds require some stimulation, grab an icelandic hot dog for only €7, a local favorite that shouldn't be missed!  are you looking for a cozy place to stay? check out kex hostel, where you can find beds for just €36 per night. this location often features a lively atmosphere with travelers from around the globe.   for affordable dining options, consider icelandic street food, where you can enjoy an all-you-can-eat soup meal with free waffles for just €20. alternatively, omnom chocolatier offers delicious treats for €12, perfect for satisfying your sweet tooth.  on a warmer day, take some time to explore the sun voyager, a stunning sculpture located by the waterfront, which is completely free to visit. don’t forget to visit hus mals og menningar bookstore, which often hosts free events!  lastly, fit in some relaxation by visiting the famous blue lagoon, but keep in mind that it comes at a price of €120. if hot springs intrigue you but not your budget, opt for smaller, less commercialized springs.  by thoughtfully planning your itinerary, you can enjoy all that reykjavik has to offer without exceeding your budget, turning your layover into a memorable adventure. whether indulging in local cuisine or simply soaking in the city's atmosphere, reykjavik on a budget offers excitement for every traveler.

🇮🇸 7 free things to do in reykjavík with kids 🇮🇸👇 i just got back from iceland with my 3 kids, and while this is definitely not a cheap destination… there are actually some really great free things to do! 👉 save this for your trip here’s what we loved: 🌈 1. walk rainbow road (skólavörðustígur) right in the center of the city, this colorful street is such a fun stop with kids—and it leads straight up to one of reykjavík’s most iconic landmarks. ⛪️ 2. visit hallgrímskirkja (outside) you don’t need to go inside to appreciate this one. the architecture is incredible, and there’s plenty of space for kids to run around (and even swings nearby!). 🌌 3. watch for the northern lights if you’re visiting at the right time of year, this is pure magic—and completely free. we kept it simple and just stepped outside when skies were clear. 🛝 4. stop at a playground reykjavík has playgrounds everywhere. this was one of the easiest ways to let the kids burn off energy between stops. 🎶 5. go inside harpa concert hall you can walk right in for free, and it’s worth it. the glass panels are designed to mimic iceland’s basalt columns, which is such a cool detail—and if you have the time, there’s a café inside. 🦆 6. feed the ducks at tjörnin this pond in the city center ended up being a surprisingly fun stop. grab a coffee, let the kids feed the ducks and geese. ⚓️ 7. visit the sun voyager this sculpture represents a viking ship and symbolizes exploration and new journeys—such a meaningful stop, and the views along the water are beautiful. ✨ other honorable mentions: • grótta lighthouse • walk around the old harbor • reykjavík city library • einar jónsson sculpture garden • heiðmörk nature reserve & rauðhólar reykjavík is surprisingly easy with kids—and these free things to do helped balance the cost of the overall trip! perfect for family trips. icelandtravel familytrip reykjavík, the capital of iceland, is often perceived as an expensive destination, especially when traveling with kids. however, there are numerous activities that cater to families without straining the budget. here are seven fantastic free things to do in reykjavík that your kids will absolutely love!  1. **walk rainbow road (skólavörðustígur)**   this vibrant street is located in the heart of the city and is perfect for a family photo op. the colorful path leads directly to hallgrímskirkja, one of reykjavík's most iconic landmarks, making it a fun stop for both walking and sightseeing.  2. **visit hallgrímskirkja (outside)**   while entering the church may have a fee, admiring its stunning architecture from the outside is completely free. the expansive area around the church is ideal for children to run off steam, with swings nearby that add to the fun.  3. **watch for the northern lights**   if your visit aligns with the right season, witnessing the northern lights is an unforgettable experience that comes at no cost. simply step outside on clear nights and enjoy the breathtaking display of nature.  4. **stop at a playground**   reykjavík is equipped with playgrounds throughout the city. these spots are perfect for letting your children burn off some energy in between sightseeing stops, making it easier to manage their enthusiasm!  5. **go inside harpa concert hall**   entry to the harpa concert hall is free, and it's an architectural marvel worth exploring. its glass panels mimic the natural basalt columns of iceland, creating a stunning visual experience. there’s also a café inside where you can take a break.  6. **feed the ducks at tjörnin**   tjörnin is a quaint pond located in downtown reykjavík, great for a casual outing. bring some bread, grab a coffee, and watch your kids delight in feeding the ducks and geese that inhabit the area.  7. **visit the sun voyager**   this iconic sculpture represents a viking ship and symbolizes exploration. situated by the water, it provides beautiful views and is a meaningful photo opportunity, captivating for both adults and children.  in addition to these seven highlights, reykjavík offers other free attractions, such as grótta lighthouse, the old harbor, the reykjavík city library, and einar jónsson sculpture garden. exploring the heiðmörk nature reserve & rauðhólar is also a refreshing experience.    with all these free activities, reykjavík turns out to be a family-friendly destination that balances enjoyment and budget. make sure to save this list for your upcoming trip and share it with fellow travelers who are looking to explore iceland with children!

seven days in iceland covering the golden circle, the snæfellsnes peninsula, the south coast, jökulsárlón glacier lagoons and þórsmörk. the itinerary lists daily drives, key attractions, recommended hotels and suggested restaurants so you can follow the same route and timing.  day 1 – golden circle & reykjavík arrive in iceland and pick up your rental car. follow the classic golden circle route: gullfoss waterfall, geysir geothermal area with strokkur erupting, and þingvellir national park (visited later on day 4 in this plan). check into hotel exeter in reykjavík and dine at lola.  day 2 – snæfellsnes peninsula (base hótel búðir) drive west to the snæfellsnes peninsula and check in at hótel búðir. key stops: ytri tunga seal beach, the búðir lava fields and black church, bæjarfoss waterfall, hellissandur and snæfellsjökull national park, and the lóndrangar basalt cliffs. dinner recommendation: matarlist.  day 3 – snæfellsnes peninsula (4x4 f570 mountain road) use a 4x4 for the f570 mountain road and scenic viewpoints. visit the lóndrangar viewpoint, the iconic kirkjufell and kirkjufellsfoss, and have lunch at harbour cafe. return to hótel búðir for the night.  day 4 – golden circle & ion adventure hotel return toward the golden circle to visit þingvellir national park. check into ion adventure hotel and relax at the lava spa. dinner at the hotel completes the day.  day 5 – south coast to jökulsárlón drive along the south coast with stops at dyrhólaey viewpoint (puffin viewing in season) and lunch at black crust pizzeria. continue east to fjallsárlón glacier lagoon and jökulsárlón glacier lagoon, then walk diamond beach. check into hotel jökulsárlón.  day 6 – southeast iceland: vestrahorn, fjaðrárgljúfur, skógafoss visit the stokksnes peninsula and vindaloo & truffle garlic butter pizza! icelandreykjavikpizzaindianfoodindonesianfood  how to make vindaloo and truffle garlic butter pizza with icelandic inspiration — step-by-step, flavors, and serving tips. iceland reykjavik pizza indianfood indonesianfood lead a fusion pizza combining spicy vindaloo and luxurious truffle garlic butter brings bold indian and gourmet flavors to a reykjavik-style pie. this guide shows what to expect from vindaloo & truffle garlic butter pizza, simple steps to recreate it, and serving suggestions inspired by iceland food scenes.  why this pizza works vindaloo’s tangy, spicy curry notes contrast beautifully with the rich, umami truffle garlic butter. the result is a layered flavor profile: heat and acidity from vindaloo balanced by buttery, aromatic truffle and garlic. that contrast makes the pizza memorable and suitable for adventurous eaters exploring iceland food trends.  ingredients overview - pizza dough (store-bought or homemade) - vindaloo sauce or cooked vindaloo (indian-style curry with vinegar, chilies, and spices) - truffle garlic butter (softened butter mixed with minced garlic and truffle oil or finely grated truffle) - mozzarella or melting cheese of choice - optional toppings: sliced onions, bell peppers, fresh cilantro, thinly sliced potatoes, or sautéed mushrooms - olive oil, salt, and pepper  quick method 1. preheat oven to a high temperature (475–500°f / 245–260°c) with a pizza stone or baking sheet inside. 2. stretch dough to desired size and thickness on a floured surface. brush the base lightly with olive oil. 3. spread a thin layer of vindaloo sauce over the dough — enough for flavor without sogginess. 4. dot or brush truffle garlic butter around the pizza, focusing on edges and spots between sauce for bursts of richness. 5. add shredded mozzarella and any optional toppings. 6. bake on the hot stone or sheet until crust is golden and cheese is bubbling (8–12 minutes depending on oven). 7. finish with a light drizzle of extra truffle oil or a sprinkle of fresh cilantro.  serving tips - slice into 6–8 pieces and serve hot. the contrast of spicy vindaloo and truffle butter is strongest right from the oven. - offer chilled yogurt or a simple cucumber raita on the side to cool the palate. - pair with a crisp icelandic lager, sparkling water with lemon, or a light white wine.  variations and notes - vegetarian: use a vegetable vindaloo or roasted cauliflower as the main topping. - heat control: adjust vindaloo spice level or dilute with tomato sauce for milder heat. - truffle substitute: premium olive oil and extra garlic can mimic some richness if truffle oil isn’t available. - local twist: add smoked lamb or icelandic seafood for a reykjavik-inspired variation.  closing takeaway vindaloo & truffle garlic butter pizza is a bold fusion that blends indian heat with gourmet truffle richness — an ideal choice for food explorers in reykjavik or anyone wanting an adventurous homemade pizza. try the balance of spicy, tangy, and buttery flavors and tweak toppings to match your heat preference.  engagement prompt which topping would you add to your vindaloo pizza — roasted cauliflower, smoked lamb, or mushrooms?

qué ver en reykjavík en un día🥶🇮🇸 reykjavik islandia iceland travel  discover the best spots to visit in reykjavik in just one day, from stunning churches to vibrant streets. reykjavik islandia iceland travel reykjavik, the charming capital of iceland, is a city rich in culture and beauty, making it a perfect destination for travelers seeking adventure in just one day. start your day at hallgrímskirkja, an iconic church that offers panoramic views of the city from its tower. don’t forget your camera as this is one of the most photographed landmarks in iceland! 10 things to do in reykjavik - iceland places to v-2 discover the essential activities and sights in reykjavik, iceland. unveil the beauty of this vibrant city! reykjavik iceland travel reykjavik, the capital of iceland, is a vibrant city boasting a rich cultural scene and stunning natural beauty. if you’re planning a trip, here are the top 10 things to do in this enchanting city.  1. **visit hallgrímskirkja**: this iconic church is one of reykjavik’s most famous landmarks. climb to the top for breathtaking panoramic views of the city and beyond.  2. **explore the harpa concert hall**: renowned for its stunning architecture and dynamic glass facade, harpa is a leading cultural venue that hosts concerts and events throughout the year.  3. **stroll along the waterfront**: reykjavik’s waterfront offers picturesque views and a pleasant walking path. check out the sun voyager sculpture, a beautiful ode to iceland’s viking past.  4. **relax in the blue lagoon**: just a short drive from reykjavik, the blue lagoon is a must-visit for its geothermal spa. relax in the mineral-rich waters and enjoy the surrounding lava field scenery.  5. **enjoy local cuisine**: sample icelandic delicacies like fresh seafood, lamb, and traditional dishes at local restaurants. don’t forget to try the classic "skyr" yogurt!  6. **visit the national museum of iceland**: dive into icelandic history through engaging exhibitions that tell the story of the country's past from the viking era to today.  7. **experience reykjavik’s nightlife**: the city is known for its vibrant nightlife. explore bars and clubs that offer live music and a lively atmosphere, particularly on weekends.  8. **discover the settlement exhibition**: this archaeological site showcases the remnants of the first known viking settler’s house, providing insight into the early days of reykjavik.  9. **take a whale watching tour**: the waters surrounding reykjavik are abundant with marine life. join a tour to spot whales and other wildlife in their natural habitat.  10. **visit the perlan museum**: this unique building houses a fantastic exhibition about glaciers and ice, including an indoor ice cave. the observation deck also offers stunning views of the city and surrounding landscape.  these activities not only showcase the beauty and culture of reykjavik but also immerse you in the unique icelandic way of life. whether it’s your first visit or a returning trip, reykjavik offers endless opportunities for adventure and relaxation.

top 5 things to do in iceland 🇮🇸  iceland reykjavík skylagoon icelandtraveltips thingstodoiceland    explore the top 5 things to do in iceland for an unforgettable adventure. perfect travel tips for your iceland trip! iceland reykjavík skylagoon icelandtraveltips thingstodoiceland iceland, a land of stunning landscapes and unique cultural experiences, offers countless adventures for travelers. here are the top five things you must do when visiting this breathtaking country.  1. **visit the blue lagoon**: a geothermal spa located in a lava field, the blue lagoon is a must-have experience in iceland. the warm, mineral-rich waters are perfect for relaxation and rejuvenation. don’t forget to book in advance, as this popular destination tends to fill up quickly!  2. **explore reykjavík**: the capital city is a vibrant hub of culture, art, and nightlife. take a stroll down laugavegur, the main shopping street, and visit landmarks like hallgrímskirkja church for panoramic views of the city. be sure to check out the local cafes and art galleries that showcase icelandic creativity.  3. **venture to the golden circle**: this popular route includes several of iceland’s top natural wonders. stop by þingvellir national park, a unesco world heritage site, where the north american and eurasian tectonic plates meet. don’t miss the powerful gullfoss waterfall and the geysers at haukadalur, particularly strokkur, which erupts every few minutes!  4. **experience the northern lights**: if you're visiting in winter, witnessing the aurora borealis is a magical experience. the best months to see this natural phenomenon are from late september to april. consider booking a guided tour to improve your chances of seeing the lights, as they often require travel away from city lights for the best visibility.  5. **discover snæfellsnes peninsula**: known as "iceland in miniature" for its diverse landscapes, this peninsula boasts stunning mountains, lava fields, and picturesque beaches. visit kirkjufell, one of iceland’s most photographed mountains, and explore the surrounding villages for a true taste of icelandic life.  these five activities will not only showcase the natural beauty of iceland but also provide unforgettable memories. whether you're soaking in the blue lagoon or searching for the northern lights, iceland is a destination that promises adventure and wonder. plan your trip today and enjoy all that this magnificent country has to offer!

a sighting can be brief. if you’re below deck or distracted, you can miss the moment.   - fatigue: long travel and early starts make dozing off likely — as noted in this trip, falling asleep meant missing visible whales.   - weather and sea conditions: rough seas or low visibility reduce chances of spotting animals.    tips to improve your experience - stay on deck when possible and watch the water with crew guidance.   - dress warmly and bring windproof layers; mornings are cold at sea.   - charge your camera and keep it ready; sightings can be quick.   - if very jet-lagged, consider a later tour or an organized transfer that lets you rest first.  practical notes for visitors arriving early in reykjavik if you land at 6am and can’t check in until the afternoon, a morning whale-watching tour is a sensible way to use time and see iceland’s coastline. keep expectations realistic about wildlife viewing, and be prepared for the possibility of limited sightings.  close a 9am whale-watching tour from reykjavik offers a chance to see marine life and make the most of an early arrival, but sightings aren’t guaranteed — staying alert on deck and planning for tiredness will improve your chances. have you ever missed a wildlife sighting while traveling? share your experience.

the blue lagoon—arguably iceland’s most famous attraction—is tucked into a jagged volcanic landscape in the middle of nowhere, about a 45-minute drive south of downtown reykjavík and not far from the international airport. people flock here from around the globe to soak, splash, and bob in the lagoon’s thermal and, yes, milky-blue waters. while many visitors consider the blue lagoon a must, it’s not everyone’s cup of tea--it’s pricey, crowded, time-consuming, and not ideal for small kids.   reservations are required, and some slots sell out days in advance. (the bottleneck isn’t the lagoon itself, but the number of lockers.) the reservation requirement keeps the lagoon from getting too congested—even on the busiest days, you can find pockets of hot water where you can escape the tour groups.   watch this full rick steves' europe episode from season 13, "iceland's reykjavík and the golden circle," on your local public television station, with pbs passport, and on our website.  ricksteves ricksteveseurope iceland bluelagoon icelandtravel  discover iceland's blue lagoon, a stunning geothermal spa with milky-blue waters. plan your visit and enjoy a serene soak! ricksteves iceland bluelagoon

10:30am and 13:30pm. i'd recommend booking onto the 10:30am slot, straight after your breakfast. that way, if there's anything you learn about on the tour that you want to add into your itinerary, you have the time to do it. although it’s advertised as free, of course you should pay what you think it’s worth at the end.  🌈 after your walking tour, take a wander down rainbow street, where you'll find loads of interesting shops, cafes, and restaurants. ⛪️ once you reach the top of rainbow street, you'll arrive at hallgrímskirkja church. the design takes inspiration from iceland's basalt columns, like the ones you'll see on reynisfjara beach. entrance to the church is free but i'd recommend paying the 4,000 isk/£8/$10 fee to take the elevator to the top for stunning panoramic views over reykjavik.   ☀️ next, head to the sun voyager statue, located on the waterfront in reykjavik, with stunning views of the sea and mountains behind it.  🎶 from the sun voyager, you can walk along the waterfront to the harpa concert hall. this impressive building overlooks the water and its' one of reykjavik's most beautiful buildings. 🍔 head to le kock for lunch, located just across the road from harpa. they do delicious burgers, wings and sides. 🐳 reykjavik has a lot of different museums and you won't be able to see them all in one day, so i'd recommend choosing just one. i visited whales of iceland, which unsurprisingly, is all about the whales that live, or pass through, iceland's waters. 🧖‍♀️ then, head to the sky lagoon for sunset, located just 15 minutes from reykjavik city centre. it's famous for its 7 step ritual that will take you through saunas, scrubs and cold plunges! 🍸 once you're back in the city and freshened up, it's time for dinner and drinks at skal! - traveltips travelguide icelandtravel reykjavik, the coolest capital on earth, is an exciting destination, but it can be costly if travelers aren't careful. here are four essential tips for anyone planning to visit.  1. **buy cheaper booze at duty-free**: if you're looking to enjoy a drink, note that buying beer in reykjavik can cost up to $12. to save money, purchase your alcohol at the airport duty-free before you leave baggage claim. you'll find much better prices here, allowing you to enjoy your drinks without breaking the bank.  2. **tipping norms in iceland**: unlike many countries, tipping in iceland is not expected. wages are generally high and service charges are included in your bill. if you wish, you can round up your bill as a gesture of appreciation, but there's no pressure to leave an extra 20%.  3. **avoid bottled water**: bottled water is not only expensive, but it's also unnecessary. the tap water in reykjavik is among the cleanest and tastiest in the world, so save your money by filling up a reusable bottle instead.  4. **affordable swimming alternatives**: while the blue lagoon is a popular attraction known for its stunning beauty, it can be overcrowded and expensive. instead, consider visiting one of reykjavik's public swimming pools, such as laugardalslaug or sundhellen. entry typically costs around $10 and includes access to hot tubs, making it a budget-friendly and enjoyable option.  for more travel tips and advice, follow @ccatravelcollective for updates on travel destinations, food, and gear. if you want a guide to help you travel smart and safe, simply send me the word "smart" in a message from my profile homepage!

🏙️ 1. hallgrímskirkja church 	•	reykjavík’s most iconic landmark. 	•	take the elevator to the top for amazing panoramic views of the city and mountains. 	•	the design is inspired by iceland’s basalt lava columns. ⸻ 🎨 2. harpa concert hall & conference centre 	•	a striking glass building on the harbor. 	•	beautiful light reflections at night. 	•	great spot for concerts, cultural events, or a scenic coffee break. ⸻ 🌊 3. the old harbor 	•	a lively waterfront area with cafés, seafood restaurants, and small museums. 	•	starting point for many boat tours. 	•	perfect for a casual stroll with sea and mountain views. ⸻ 🐋 4. whale watching & puffin tours 	•	tours depart directly from the old harbor. 	•	whale sightings year-round; puffins best seen may–september. 	•	dress warmly — it’s breezy out there! ⸻ 🧊 5. perlan (the pearl) 	•	futuristic glass dome with an incredible 360° observation deck. 	•	features a man-made ice cave and glacier exhibition. 	•	northern lights planetarium show is a highlight on cloudy days. ⸻ 🧖 6. sky lagoon 	•	a modern geothermal spa with ocean views. 	•	infinity-edge lagoon and a deeply relaxing 7-step ritual. 	•	close to downtown but feels like a retreat. ⸻ ❄️ 7. ride a snowmobile 	•	one of iceland’s most thrilling adventures. 	•	usually done on langjökull glacier or as part of a golden circle + snowmobile combo tour. 	•	incredible sweeping views of endless ice fields — like being on another planet. 	•	all gear is provided; no experience needed. ⸻ 🧭 8. sun voyager sculpture (sólfar) 	•	stainless-steel viking-ship-inspired sculpture on the waterfront. 	•	symbolizes exploration and hope. 	•	beautiful at sunrise or sunset. ⸻ 🍻 9. laugavegur street 	•	reykjavík’s main shopping and nightlife street. 	•	filled with boutiques, craft beer bars, coffee shops, and murals. 	•	great place to try icelandic lamb soup or grab a local brew. ⸻ 🌋 10. day trip to the golden circle 	•	classic must-see route easily done from reykjavík: 	•	þingvellir national park 	•	geysir hot springs 	•	gullfoss waterfall 	•	easy to combine with snowmobiling for an epic full-day adventure. northernlights reykjavik iceland snow traveltiktok

join me as i explore iceland’s ringroad starting with the goldencircle from reykjavik to vik  join the journey through iceland’s golden circle, featuring breathtaking sites from reykjavík to vik. discover waterfalls, geysers, and stunning landscapes! iceland ringroad goldencircle iceland's ring road is a breathtaking journey that showcases some of the most stunning landscapes and natural wonders in the country. day 1 of this adventure starts with the golden circle, an iconic route that takes travelers from reykjavík to vik, laden with historical, geological, and photographic treasures.  commencing in reykjavík around 8 am, the first stop is þingvallavatn lake, iceland's largest natural lake. this beautiful body of water is nestled within þingvellir national park, a unesco world heritage site that holds significance as the location of one of the world's first democratic parliaments. not only is this a historical site, but it also features a dramatic geological phenomenon where the american and eurasian tectonic plates drift apart. visitors will find various trails that lead through stunning landscapes, though it's essential to heed the park’s somber history where women faced severe punishments.  next up is gullfoss, one of iceland's most famous waterfalls, followed by the strokkur geyser. known for its reliability, strokkur erupts approximately every 7 minutes, providing a great opportunity for visitors to witness the raw power of nature. although you may miss the grander eruptions, you can still spend a delightful hour exploring the area and enjoying the viewpoints.  seljalandsfoss is another highlight on this route. famous for the fact that you can walk behind it, waterproofing your gear is a wise decision for those wanting to get up close! just a short drive away is skógafoss, a magnificent waterfall that also marks the trailhead for fimmvorduhals, a famed hiking route featuring multiple falls.  finally, the journey moves on to reynisfjara, renowned for its striking black sand beaches and the impressive basalt columns that rise like natural sculptures from the shore. keep in mind that changing weather and storms have altered a lot of the coastline, so caution is advised when exploring.  from þingvellir's remarkable tectonic activity to the roaring waterfalls and enchanting beaches, day 1 of the icelandic ring road adventure is an unforgettable experience. a perfect way to conclude the day is in the coastal town of vik, ready for the next day's exploration. whether you're an avid traveler or simply an admirer of nature’s beauty, iceland promises an enchanting journey.

my sister and i went to iceland for the first time in may. here are some things you should know before your trip:  1) the weather changes fast! it was warm most of our trip, but the last couple of days were cold. make sure you bring layers and be prepared for all types of weather.  2) everything is expensive! we stayed in hostels and were able to cook our own meals. 3) go to the popular spots later in the day. the tour buses were gone around dinner. we went back to a couple of the big waterfalls for sunset and were the only ones there.  4) don’t speed! there’s cameras to monitor speeding. they usually warn you before with a sign on the side of the road.  5) bring a rain jacket! the bottom of the waterfalls get misty.  have fun!! we had a great time! traveltiktok iceland waterfall traveltips

must-visit perfumery in reykjavik 🇮🇸 if you’re a fragrance lover visiting reykjavik, iceland… you need to add fischersund to your itinerary! i have taken a ‘scent tour’ there and visited on other occasions and it is one of my favorite things i have ever done in the city. the tours are 3,999 isk (about $32) and you get a 30 minute guided tour of their flagship store (located at fischersund 3), a small gift to bring home, and some of their signature icelandic schnapps. inside you’ll find a variety of unique fragrances made from icelandic herbs and oils. i purchased three of them (and some samples) and nothing in my collection smells remotely similar to any of them. another fun fact is that this is a family-owned business and one of them is in the famous icelandic band sigur rós. the creative atmosphere and usage of sound inside the store really puts all your senses to work in the most immersive journey i have ever had inside a perfumery! you can sign up for a tour on their website and they run daily from 12pm until 4pm. otherwise, you can visit their store m-f from 11am to 6pm, saturday from 11am to 5pm, and sunday from 11am to 4pm. reykjavik iceland perfumetok | things to do in reykjavik | reykjavik itinerary | perfumes in iceland | icelandic perfumes explore fischersund in reykjavik for a unique scent tour, featuring icelandic fragrances and a delightful experience! reykjavik iceland perfumetok

make sure to add kerlingarfjöll to your iceland itinerary 🇮🇸 the roads are challenging so exploring with @arcticadventures is the best way to experience this unique destination ⛰️ hiking in the colorful mountains and geothermal valleys makes you feel like you are on another planet 🥾😍 centerhotels iceland visiticeland icelandadventure arcticadventures  plan a kerlingarfjöll visit with arctic adventures — hike vivid geothermal valleys and rugged trails safely. tips for adding kerlingarfjöll to your iceland itinerary. centerhotels iceland visiticeland icelandadventure arcticadventures lead kerlingarfjöll is a striking geothermal mountain range in central iceland known for colorful rhyolite peaks, steaming hot springs and otherworldly valleys. adding kerlingarfjöll to your iceland itinerary rewards you with dramatic hikes and landscapes that feel like another planet. because access involves rough highland roads, exploring with a guided operator such as arctic adventures is the recommended and safest option.  why kerlingarfjöll belongs on your iceland itinerary - unique landscape: vivid, multicolored rhyolite mountains, geothermal vents, and steaming valleys offer photo opportunities and a landscape unlike coastal iceland. - solitude and nature: fewer visitors than popular ring-road sites, so hikes feel remote and immersive. - seasonal access: roads are challenging and typically open only in summer; guided trips handle logistics and safety.  getting there and safety - road conditions: highland tracks leading to kerlingarfjöll are rough and often require a 4x4. weather can change rapidly. independent travel is possible only with proper vehicles and experience. - guided option: booking with arctic adventures or a similar guide provides transport, local route knowledge, and safety equipment — the most practical way for most travelers.  hiking and highlights - colorful mountains: easy-to-moderate hiking trails traverse rhyolite slopes with reds, yellows and browns formed by geothermal activity. - geothermal valleys: steaming vents and hot springs punctuate valleys—observe from marked paths and follow guide instructions. - trail tips: wear sturdy hiking boots, layered clothing, and bring water and snacks. expect mud and uneven terrain.  suggested itinerary ideas - day trip: join a guided day tour from reykjavík or nearby hubs to see the main geothermal areas and complete one or two short hikes. - multi-day stay: if available, stay at local mountain huts or campsites to explore further and enjoy sunrise/sunset light on the rhyolite peaks.  what to pack - waterproof hiking boots and gaiters - warm, layered clothing and windproof outer layer - hat, gloves and sunscreen - water, snacks, and camera - small first-aid kit  closing / call to action include kerlingarfjöll on your next iceland itinerary for a rare, otherworldly hike through colorful geothermal mountains. for safer, more convenient access and local knowledge of road and trail conditions, book a guided trip with arctic adventures. have you added kerlingarfjöll to your route? share which iceland stops you’re pairing it with.

the best excuse ever to stay up past midnight 🐣 one of the most unusual wildlife experiences in iceland, and it only happens for a few weeks every year on the westman islands. 📍 heimaey, vestmannaeyjar, iceland baby puffins leaving their burrows mistake the town's streetlights for the moon and crash-land in the streets. locals have run night rescue patrols for generations, and every rescued bird is checked at the aquarium and released off the cliffs the next morning. here are 4 tips for joining the puffling patrol: 1. the season runs roughly late august to mid september, peak in the first weeks of september. 2. check in at the sea life trust aquarium first, they run the rescue programme, log every bird, and can point you to a morning release. 3. bring a torch and a cardboard box and search near streetlights and the harbour after dark. 4. getting there is a 35-minute ferry from landeyjahöfn or a short flight from reykjavík, and beds book out in season. send this video to the mio app and it'll save heimaey straight onto your travel map. pufflingpatrol westmanislands icelandtravel puffins wildlifeencounters

a few things to know before visiting the blue lagoon in iceland. 🚌 getting there: you can take a bus straight from keflavík airport to the blue lagoon - perfect for day 1 or your last day. the lagoon is 20 min from the airport, 45 min from reykjavik. book shuttle buses in advance online. 🧳 luggage storage: coming straight from the airport? they have luggage storage available so you can store your bags while you soak, then continue to reykjavik after. 🎫 booking: book tickets days or weeks in advance - they sell out. timed entry slots. go early morning (around 8am) for fewer crowds. ⚠️ hair: do not get your hair wet. the geothermal minerals (silica) will destroy it. tie it up in a high bun, slick it back, braid it. ✨ what's included: lagoon access, saunas, steam rooms, three face masks and a drink.  🎒 what to bring: bathing suit, flip-flops (towels provided), hair tie, waterproof phone case for photos. 💰 cost: $103-197 usd depending on admission level. 📍 blue lagoon, iceland bluelagoon iceland geothermalspa traveltips

sundays may have reduced hours. plan shopping for mid-day to late afternoon.  - payment: most shops accept credit/debit cards; contactless payment is common. carry a small amount of local currency only if you prefer cash.  - packing: choose compact, well-wrapped souvenirs—wool items compress well, but fragile ceramics need cushioning.  - time management: if your day 5 is short, prioritise one or two shops or the main street to avoid rushing.  closing / call to action make the most of day 5 in akureyri by focusing on authentic icelandic items—wool, local design, and food gifts. which souvenirs matter most to you: practical outdoor gear, crafts, or edible gifts? share your pick and plan a simple route through town to save time.

holding on to that chain when it's 43° and raining is not for the faint of heart iceland esjan hiking icelandtravel reykjavik   tips for locking in to esjan's chain section during a 43° rainy solo hike in iceland. practical safety and gear suggestions for reykjavik-day hikers. iceland esjan hiking reykjavik lead reaching the chain section on esjan during a solo hike in 43° and rain is a critical moment: you must decide to lock in and continue or turn back to avoid risk and headlines. this guide focuses on practical, realistic actions to help you hold the chain, stay safe, and know when to stop.  body assess conditions immediately: note temperature (43°), steady rain, wind, and visibility. wet metal and rock become slippery; cold lowers grip strength. if wind gusts are strong or visibility drops, prioritize retreat.  grip and positioning: face the rock where possible and keep three points of contact—two feet and one hand on the chain, or two hands and one foot—while moving slowly. keep your body close to the rock to lower leverage on the chain.  gear checklist: wear waterproof gloves with good grip (thin liners under waterproof shells help maintain dexterity), waterproof hiking boots with sticky soles, and a rain shell with a hood. a harness and via ferrata lanyard clipped to the chain gives protection if available and you know how to use it.  technique for wet chain sections: move deliberately, test each step and handhold before committing weight, avoid pulling solely on the chain—transfer load to rock where possible, and keep your feet planted before reaching for the next hold. short, controlled movements reduce slip risk.  solo-hiking precautions: tell someone your plan and expected return, carry a charged phone and portable battery, and consider a personal locator or emergency beacon if you’re beyond quick assistance. if conditions worsen, opt to turn back—solo rescue on esjan in rain is risky and may end up on icelandic news.  when to stop and turn back: if the chain is submerged, moving water is present, your hands numb from cold, or you feel unsure at any point, descend. there is no shame in choosing safety over summit photos.  close / call-to-action locking in on esjan’s chain in 43° rain demands respect for the mountain and clear-headed choices. use the gear and techniques above, communicate your plan, and prioritize getting down safely. if you’ve faced this section, what grip or gear tip helped you most? share your experience for other reykjavik hikers.

don’t miss out snæfellsnes peninsula in iceland as they call it the mini iceland and perfect for a day trip from reykjavik  discover the 'mini iceland' of the snæfellsnes peninsula. perfect for a day trip from reykjavik, explore kirkjufell, stunning viewpoints, and historical sites. the snæfellsnes peninsula, often called 'mini iceland,' offers a perfect day trip experience from reykjavik. this region showcases a diverse range of iceland's iconic landscapes, condensed into a single accessible area.   your journey might begin with a stop in borgarnes, a town situated approximately 74km from reykjavik, serving as a gateway to the peninsula. from there, you'll head towards the snæfellsnes peninsula itself. along the way, discover natural wonders like selvallavatn view point and the picturesque selvallafoss.  cross the kolgrafarfjörður, also known as the sword bridge, as you make your way to grundarfjörður. here, you can witness the iconic kirkjufell, a 463m mountain famously recognized as the 'arrowhead mountain' from game of thrones. nearby, kirkjufellsfoss waterfall provides stunning photo opportunities.  continuing your exploration deeper into the peninsula, búlandshöfði view point offers dramatic coastal vistas. don't miss ingjaldshólskirkja, noted as the oldest concrete church in iceland.  at djúpalónssandur, explore the remnants of old shipwrecks and discover the famous "lifting stones," historically used by fishermen to test their strength. the coastal trail from arnarstapi presents amazing views, including the unique rock formation gatklettur. along the coast, you may also find opportunities for seal spotting at ytri-tunga.  the snæfellsnes peninsula truly offers a concentrated taste of iceland's natural beauty and rich history, making it an essential destination for any visitor based in reykjavik.

my honest take on the blue lagoon in iceland and why i would not return 🫪❄️🥶 bluelagoon icelandtravel icelandtrip traveltiktok honestreview  an honest review of the blue lagoon in iceland — what disappointed me, practical tips, and who might still enjoy it. bluelagoon icelandtravel honestreview lead this is an honest take on the blue lagoon in iceland and why i would not return. the video explains personal disappointments, expectations versus reality, and practical points other travelers should know before booking.  what disappointed me - overcrowding: the lagoon felt busy, which reduced the relaxing atmosphere expected from a geothermal spa. long lines and many visitors made privacy and quiet difficult. - commercial feel: the site has a resort, restaurants and retail that can make the experience feel tourist-focused rather than natural and serene. - cost versus value: entry fees, add-ons and extras (towels, drinks, spa treatments) raised the total cost beyond initial expectations. - water experience: while the milky-blue water is distinctive, some parts felt artificial or churned by crowds; it didn’t match the calm, untouched image often promoted. - logistics: transportation schedules, timed entry, and the need to arrive at specific slots create planning constraints some travelers might find inconvenient.  what worked well - easy access: close to keflavík airport and reykjavík, it’s straightforward to include on a short iceland itinerary. - unique setting: geothermal water and silica-rich mud provide a recognizable experience many first-time visitors appreciate. - facilities: clean changing rooms, showers, and on-site services are convenient for guests who want a full-service visit.  who might still enjoy it - first-time iceland visitors who want an iconic, easy-to-reach geothermal experience. - travelers who prefer full-service resorts with on-site dining and spa treatments. - visitors who book off-peak times, private options, or premium packages to reduce crowds.  practical tips before you go - book tickets in advance and check entry time windows. - compare package options — entry-only versus treatments — to see which offers best value for your needs. - visit at quieter times (early morning or late evening) if possible to avoid peak crowds. - bring necessary items or confirm what’s included (some packages exclude towels or robes). - consider alternatives: less-commercial geothermal pools and natural hot springs around iceland for a quieter experience.  takeaway the blue lagoon delivers a recognizable, convenient geothermal attraction, but the commercial atmosphere, crowds, and total cost mean it may not meet expectations for travelers seeking a peaceful, natural hot-spring experience. decide based on what you value most: convenience and iconic imagery, or tranquility and authenticity.  question for readers would you choose the blue lagoon for convenience and an iconic iceland photo, or pick a quieter natural hot spring instead? share your experience or plans below.

a stop on iceland’s golden circle that you definitely don’t want to skip 🍅 this is one of the country’s most unique dining experiences and it’s located only 1.5 hours from reykjavik 🚗 friðheimar is a family-owned tomato farm and restaurant where you'll enjoy lunch surrounded by thousands of tomato plants growing year-round using iceland's geothermal energy and sustainable greenhouse practices 🍅 their famous all-you-can-eat tomato soup served with freshly baked bread is a must, but the menu also features fresh pasta, burrata, tomato beer, bloody marys, and even tomato-inspired desserts 🥖🥫 a short walk from their main restaurant is where you’ll find their wine bar & bistro with grape vines hanging from the ceiling 🍷  be sure to make a reservation in advance, especially during the busy summer season, and don't forget to stop by the little tomato shop before you leave for locally made sauces, jams, and other delicious souvenirs. *** tip: if you aren’t able to get a table at the main restaurant, head over to the wine bar where you can still order off the food menu! trust us—this is one of those iceland experiences you'll be talking about long after your trip. 🍅🇮🇸 visiticeland goldencircle traveliceland iceland icelandtravel  discover friðheimar: geothermal tomato greenhouses, all-you-can-eat tomato soup, pasta, tomato beer, wine bar & little tomato shop 1.5 hrs from reykjavík. visiticeland goldencircle lead friðheimar is a family-owned tomato farm and restaurant on iceland’s golden circle, about 1.5 hours from reykjavík. the dining room sits surrounded by thousands of tomato plants grown year-round using geothermal energy and sustainable greenhouse practices, making it one of iceland’s most unique dining experiences.  what to expect - atmosphere: lunch among tomato plants in greenhouses heated by iceland’s geothermal energy. the setting is photogenic and unlike a typical restaurant. - signature dish: all-you-can-eat tomato soup served with freshly baked bread — a must-try and the restaurant’s most famous offering. - menu highlights: fresh pasta, burrata, tomato beer, bloody marys, and tomato-inspired desserts. if the main restaurant is full, the nearby wine bar & bistro serves items from the food menu. - wine bar & bistro: a short walk from the main restaurant, with grape vines hanging from the ceiling and a relaxed bar menu.  practical tips - reservations: book in advance, especially during the busy summer season. tables at the main restaurant fill up quickly. - alternative seating: if you can’t get a main-restaurant table, visit the wine bar — you can still order from the food menu there. - location: approximately 1.5 hours’ drive from reykjavík, convenient as a golden circle stop. - little tomato shop: before leaving, stop by for locally made sauces, jams, and souvenirs to take home.  why visit friðheimar combines sustainable agriculture, geothermal energy use, and creative tomato-focused cuisine in a memorable setting. the experience—from soup to tomato beer to the greenhouse dining room—is distinctive and highly shareable.  closing / call to action plan ahead and reserve a table to enjoy friðheimar’s tomato soup, pasta and tomato drinks. if your schedule is tight, allow time to visit the wine bar and little tomato shop so you don’t miss this golden circle highlight.

iceland 🇮🇸 blue lagoon 🩵pakistan viral husbandwife fyp foryoupage  practical tips for visiting iceland’s blue lagoon—tickets, what to bring, best times, and highlights from a visitor’s perspective. pakistan viral husbandwife lead blue lagoon iceland is a world-famous geothermal spa known for milky-blue water set in a lava field outside reykjavík. this concise guide gives practical, visitor-focused details on tickets, essentials to bring, best times to visit, and what to expect once you arrive.  what the blue lagoon is the blue lagoon is a geothermal bathing complex fed by mineral-rich seawater from a nearby geothermal power plant. visitors come for the warm, milky-blue water, silica mud, steam saunas, and the dramatic lava-field setting.  tickets and booking - reserve tickets in advance: entrance is capacity-limited and often sells out. prebook for specific time slots.  - compare ticket types: basic entrance vs. packages that include towel, robe, drinks, or spa treatments. choose based on how long you plan to stay and whether you want extras.  what to bring - swimsuit and waterproof bag for valuables. - hair tie and conditioner: silica can dry hair; most visitors use conditioner or the complimentary conditioner stations. - flip-flops or water shoes for walking areas. - optional: towel or upgrade to a package that includes one.  best times to visit - early morning or late afternoon can be less crowded; mid-day is busiest.  - consider weather and daylight: winter offers dramatic light and possible northern lights sightings; summer has long daylight hours.  on-site tips and highlights - silica mud: apply the white silica mud to your face before rinsing for the classic blue lagoon experience. - indoor and outdoor areas: explore steam rooms and saunas as well as the outdoor lagoon. - food and drink: there are cafes and a restaurant on-site; some packages include a drink voucher. - photos: the blue water and black lava create striking photos—respect other guests when taking pictures.  accessibility and facilities - the site provides changing rooms, lockers, showers, and drying areas. check accessibility options in advance if you have mobility needs.  nearby practicalities - location: near keflavík international airport and within easy reach of reykjavík; many visitors combine a blue lagoon visit with arrival or departure travel plans. - transportation: shuttle buses, private transfers, or rental car options are commonly used.  closing / takeaway visiting the blue lagoon is about planning: book tickets ahead, bring essentials like a swimsuit and conditioner, and pick a time of day that fits your crowd and lighting preferences. whether part of a longer iceland trip or a stop near the airport, the blue lagoon offers a unique geothermal spa experience set in iconic icelandic scenery.  engagement prompt have you visited the blue lagoon or planning a trip? share your experience or questions about tickets, packing, or timing.

🌋 this lava cave is just 30 minutes from reykjavík… and somehow still feels like a hidden gem 🌋👇 📌 save this post for your iceland trip 📌  we visited the raufarhólshellir lava tunnel, and this ended up being one of the coolest (❄️ pun intended) experiences of our entire iceland trip. this cave was formed over 5,000 years ago when lava flowed through and hardened, so you’re actually walking through the exact path that molten rock carved. it’s wild. and if you’re picturing something tight and claustrophobic—this is not that. it’s one of the longest lava tubes in iceland (about 1,360 meters), with huge open spaces, colorful mineral walls, and skylights that let in natural light. in the winter, you’ll even see ice formations inside ❄️ a few things to know before you go: ✨ cost: ~7,500–8,000 isk per adult (kids discounted) ✨ reservations are strongly recommended (this fills up!) ✨ guided tour is required—and worth it ✨ tour is about 1 hour inside the cave ✨ you can book a tour from reykjavík or drive yourself (super easy access) and definitely: ✔️ dress warm (it’s around 4°c / 39°f year-round) ✔️ wear sturdy shoes (uneven ground in spots) this tour is unique to iceland—and it’s such an easy add from reykjavík. raufarholshellir lavatunnel icelandcaves icelandtravel visiticeland

well, i clearly did not read the website💀 the blue lagoon’s seawater contains high levels of silica and other minerals. when those minerals build up on your hair, they can leave it feeling incredibly stiff and weirdly plasticky…? to help prevent this, the blue lagoon recommends coating your hair in conditioner before getting in, leaving it in while you swim, and tying long hair up to minimize contact with the water. if you do fully soak your hair (like i did), be prepared for it to take a few days, several washes, and a stupid amount of conditioner before it starts feeling normal again.😂  the good news is, despite the horrifying texture, the plasticky effect is only temporary. so don’t worry, your hair isn’t permanently damaged.😅🙌🏻 iceland bluelagoon solotravel icelandtravel worldtravel

don’t miss the second tallest waterfall in iceland 🇮🇸  📍glymur waterfall, located just a little over an hour from reykjavik  at almost 200 meters tall (🤯), glymur was considered the tallest waterfall in the country for centuries, until a slightly taller cascade was uncovered in 2007, due to glacial melt. regardless of losing its title, glymur is still absolutely stunning, tucked away in a mossy canyon with hundreds of fulmars (an arctic seabird) swooping through its mist.  here’s what to know about visiting the glymur waterfall:  🥾 to reach the top of the waterfall, you have to hike a challenging 4.5 mile trail, with 1,400 feet of elevation gain. the trail is pretty fun, with a log bridge river crossing and a cool cave along the way!  🗓️ the log bridge to cross the botsná river is in place from late may through october. while the trail is technically open when the log bridge is removed from october through may, it’s extremely dangerous to cross the river without it, due to the strong current and icy water—so i’d strongly advise against it! 👣 the trail is usually hiked as a loop, which requires wading through an ankle deep section of the river after you reach the waterfall. so bring an extra pair of socks and shoes in the car to change out of when you’re done—or, if you don’t feel like getting wet, do what we did and just turn around after you reach the waterfall!  🚗 parking is free, which is hard to find in iceland!!  🔥 consider stopping at hvammsvik hot springs after your hike—it’s a beautiful series of pools overlooking the surrounding lush green mountains of hvalfjörður.  is glymur on your iceland bucketlist?  glymur iceland icelandtravel  explore glymur, iceland’s dramatic 200m waterfall—challenging 4.5mi hike, log bridge season, free parking, and hvammsvik hot springs nearby. glymur iceland icelandtravel glymur waterfall stands as one of iceland’s most dramatic natural sights. at nearly 200 meters, it was long considered the country’s tallest cascade until 2007, when glacial melt revealed a slightly taller fall. even without the title, glymur remains stunning—tucked in a mossy canyon where fulmars swoop through mist and the landscape feels remote and wild.  what to expect on the trail - distance and difficulty: the hike to the top is a challenging 4.5 mile route with about 1,400 feet of elevation gain. expect rocky switchbacks and steady climbing; this is a hike for prepared walkers rather than casual strollers. - trail features: the route is engaging, including a log-bridge crossing over the botsná river and a cool cave along the way. the loop normally requires wading through an ankle-deep section of river after reaching the waterfall. - seasonal river crossing: the log bridge is installed from late may through october. outside those months the trail remains technically open, but crossing the botsná without the bridge is extremely dangerous because of strong currents and icy water—avoid attempting it when the bridge is absent.  practical tips - footwear and extras: bring sturdy hiking shoes and an extra pair of socks and shoes to change into after the river crossing. if you prefer to stay dry, consider turning around at the waterfall viewpoint instead of completing the full loop. - parking: parking at glymur is free, a welcome rarity in iceland. - nearby stops: after the hike, consider a soak at hvammsvik hot springs—beautiful pools overlooking the green mountains of hvalfjörður, a perfect way to relax sore legs.  takeaway glymur offers a rewarding, adventurous day trip from reykjavík (just over an hour away) for hikers who want dramatic scenery without the crowds of more famous sites. plan your visit during late spring to early autumn for the safe log-bridge crossing, pack extra socks, and add hvammsvik hot springs to your itinerary for a complete icelandic outing.  is glymur on your iceland bucket list? share your plans or questions about the trail and seasonal access.

its easier than you think to get from the keflavik airport to downtown reykjavik without a car.  arcticspiral visiticeland iceland icelandadventure solotravel  learn how to easily travel from keflavik airport to downtown reykjavik using the fly bus and color-coded buses. avoid the hassle and enjoy a smooth transfer! arcticspiral visiticeland iceland icelandadventure solotravel traveling from keflavik airport to downtown reykjavik is a breeze, even without a car! 🚍✨ here’s a simple guide to help you navigate the journey effortlessly.  upon exiting keflavik airport, you'll immediately spot the flybus service, which is perfectly timed with flight arrivals—especially useful for early morning landings from the us. 🛬 don’t worry about long waits; buses arrive every hour! you can choose to purchase your tickets either in advance or upon arrival.  the scenic ride to reykjavik takes around 45 minutes, and you’ll be dropped off at the city’s bus terminal. however, this isn't your final stop. due to their size, the large buses can’t access downtown reykjavik directly. instead, you will transfer to a smaller bus that will take you closer to your hotel.  when boarding the smaller bus, staff will ask for your hotel details and provide you with a color-coded card. simply find the bus that matches the color of your card, and it will take you to your designated stop. from there, just a short walk will lead you to your accommodation.   it's that easy to navigate your way into the heart of reykjavik! enjoy your adventure in this stunning icelandic city! 🌌🏙️  keflavikairport reykjavik icelandadventure solotravel visiticeland traveltips

tutti parlano dell’islanda di cosa vedere e cosa fare di qua e di là, ma pochi, anzi pochissimi, parlano della capitale: il luogo da cui parte ogni viaggio in islanda🇮🇸 reykjavik, l’unica “grande” città dell’isola, è il luogo dove la maggior parte dei turisti passa, ma di cui viene parlato veramente poco nei social. la capitale islandese non è di certo una città must da vedere nella vita come londra, parigi o roma, ma ha anche lei le sue bellezze e caratteristiche che la rendono molto carina. 🚌 per chi non volesse fare un tour on the road, la maggior parte dei tour della costa ovest, sud e nord sono fattibile in giornata da reykjavik e normalmente i bus turistici partono dalla fermata 12, dalla hallgrimskirkja o dalla fermata centrale dei bus. ⛴️ per i tour in mare aperto per vedere sia i puffin che le balene, la partenza è sempre dal porto cittadino a due passi dall’opera e dal centro storico. qui si ha l’imbarazzo della scelta per come poter vedere queste creature del mare: battello, barca e gommone sono quasi sempre disponibili a seconda sia del tempo atmosferico (che in islanda è molto variabile) che del proprio budget (l’islanda è inutile nasconderlo, è carissima) ecco un tour di un giorno di reykjavik 📍hallgrimskirkja 📍rainbow street 📍sun voyager 📍harpa concert hall 📍ufficio del primo ministro 📍parlamento 📍ingólfstorg 📍porto  curiosità: a reykjavik è possibile visitare anche vari musei dedicati alla storia di questa città e del primo parlamento al mondo (alþingi) ma anche riguardanti temi particolari come il museo del pene, il museo del punk e il museo del nonsense. 🌋 museo da non perdere è il lava museum, uno dei due unici musei al mondo (l’altro si trova sempre in islanda a vík y mírdal nel sud dell’isola) in cui i visitatori possono assistere a giochi ed esibizioni fatti da professionisti come fossero un vero e proprio spettacolo artistico-teatrale! e tu conoscevi reykjavik? 🌍✨ ¡descubre reikiavik! la capital más septentrional del mundo 🌌  ¿sabías que reikiavik, la vibrante capital de islandia, es una ciudad llena de sorpresas y maravillas? aquí te dejo algunos datos fascinantes que te harán querer visitarla: 1. **la ciudad del vapor** 🌋: su nombre significa "bahía humeante" debido a las columnas de vapor de las aguas termales cercanas. ¡perfecto para un baño relajante en la famosa laguna azul! 💦💙 2. **tierra de luz y oscuridad** 🌅🌌: durante el verano, disfruta del sol de medianoche con días que parecen no acabar. en invierno, la aurora boreal ilumina el cielo con un espectáculo natural impresionante. 🌠 3. **cultura y arte en cada rincón** 🎨🎭: reikiavik es un hervidero de creatividad. desde su vibrante escena musical, hogar de artistas como björk y sigur rós, hasta su innovador arte callejero. ¡cada calle es una galería! 🎶🎤 4. **historia y modernidad** 🏛️🏙️: fundada en 1786, esta ciudad combina la rica historia vikinga con una arquitectura moderna impresionante, como la iglesia hallgrímskirkja y el moderno harpa concert hall. ⛪🎼 5. **sostenibilidad** 🌱♻️: reikiavik es una de las ciudades más verdes del mundo, utilizando energía geotérmica y eólica para casi todas sus necesidades energéticas. ¡un ejemplo a seguir en sostenibilidad! 🌿🌍 explorar reikiavik es como viajar a otro mundo, lleno de naturaleza, cultura y magia. ¡no te pierdas la oportunidad de conocer esta joya del norte! 🗺️❤️ iceland reikiavik islandia reykjavik viajes sostenibilidad arteycultura turismo nordicoa aventuras exploraelmundo traveltiktok

expressionist architecture is an early 20th-century movement that emphasized emotion, individuality, and the expressive power of form over strict functional or historical conventions. emerging primarily in germany during the 1910s–1930s, it explored dramatic shapes, experimental materials, and sculptural compositions to create buildings that conveyed ideas, identity, and human experience. expressionist architects viewed architecture as more than a functional object; they used form, material, and space to evoke emotion and communicate deeper cultural or symbolic meanings. a later example reflecting many expressionist principles is hallgrímskirkja in reykjavík, iceland, designed by icelandic architect guðjón samúelsson in 1937 and built between 1945 and 1986. the lutheran church was named after the 17th-century icelandic poet and clergyman hallgrímur pétursson. created as both a church and a national landmark, the design sought to express icelandic identity rather than imitate european traditions. its monumental concrete form, inspired by iceland’s basalt columns and volcanic landscape, creates a powerful connection between architecture and nature. the stepped façade, vertical geometry, and towering presence transform geological influences into a symbolic architectural expression of place. here are just a few other sights to visit when in reykjavik: walk along the harbor front, visit the harpa concert hall, relax in a geothermal spa (sky or blue lagoon), and shop / stroll along laugavegur or “wash road.” ~poché: the space between. icelandicarchitecture visitreykjavík architecture churcharchitecture visiticeland

meet svartifoss 💦💧 this is one of the places of my trip in iceland that keeps me wanting to come back.  travel iceland reykjavik svartifoss diamondbeach  explore svartifoss in skaftafell: why this basalt-column waterfall is a must-see on any iceland trip. travel tips and highlights. travel iceland svartifoss reykjavik diamondbeach lead svartifoss in skaftafell is one of iceland’s most impressive waterfalls. famous for its striking basalt columns and clear cascades, it’s a highlight many travelers return to on repeat trips around iceland.  why svartifoss stands out svartifoss is known for the regular, dark basalt columns that frame the falling water, creating a dramatic geometric backdrop. the contrast between the black rock and white water makes it a top photography spot and a memorable natural landmark on south iceland itineraries.  how visitors experience it most visitors reach svartifoss from the skaftafell visitor area. a well-marked hiking trail leads through birch and moss-covered terrain, offering scenic viewpoints before the final approach. the trail is accessible for most moderate hikers and rewards walkers with progressively better views of the waterfall and its basalt formations.  nearby highlights svartifoss often pairs well with other south iceland stops — notably diamond beach — making a compact route for those based in or near reykjavik. combining svartifoss with glacier views and coastal black-sand shores creates a varied day of landscapes.  practical tips - plan for changing weather and bring waterproof layers; conditions can shift quickly.   - wear sturdy shoes for the trail; some sections can be uneven or wet.   - visit early or late in the day for fewer crowds and softer light for photos.   - combine svartifoss with nearby attractions like diamond beach for an efficient itinerary.  takeaway svartifoss remains a must-see on any iceland trip thanks to its basalt columns and photogenic cascade. whether you’re focused on photography or simply soaking in dramatic scenery, it’s easy to understand why travelers keep returning.  engagement prompt have you visited svartifoss or skaftafell? share your best photo or tip for first-time visitors.

 📍 **relax at sky lagoon**: skip the blue lagoon and head to sky lagoon instead! located just a 10-minute taxi ride from downtown, sky lagoon offers a tranquil spa experience. i highly recommend indulging in the seven-step spa ritual—it's the perfect way to unwind after a day of exploring.  📍 **chase the northern lights**: one of the most magical experiences in iceland is witnessing the northern lights. it’s relatively easy to see them from reykjavik or nearby areas. for an incredible viewing spot, consider taking a short taxi ride to grotta lighthouse, where the darkness of the surroundings enhances your chances of catching this breathtaking spectacle.  📍 **savor an icelandic hot dog**: a trip to reykjavik wouldn't be complete without a stop at bæjarins beztu pylsur—the hot dog stand that everyone raves about! looking for that winter escape?  a weekend in reykjavik is the perfect escape for those looking to trade the ordinary for something unforgettable. wander the colourful streets of iceland’s charming capital, soak in geothermal waters, taste fresh local cuisine, and use the city as your gateway to some of nature’s most dramatic landscapes. in just a few days you can chase waterfalls, explore volcanic landscapes, walk along black sand beaches, witness steaming geysers and, if you’re lucky, dance beneath the northern lights. iceland feels like stepping onto another planet — wild, rugged and breathtakingly beautiful — making it the perfect weekend adventure for couples, friends or solo travellers looking to reset and reconnect with nature.

🇮🇸 thinking about visiting iceland? here are the tips i’d give a friend before they book. ✨ my must-do itinerary: 📍reykjavík 📍golden circle 📍south coast 📍jökulsárlón glacier lagoon & diamond beach 📍a geothermal lagoon (i loved sky lagoon!) 💡 insider tips: • stay at least 5–7 days if you can. don’t try to cram everything into one trip. • rent your car when you’re leaving reykjavík (if you visit there) you don’t need one while exploring the city. • start the golden circle early (or drive it in reverse) to beat the tour buses. • stay overnight on the south coast instead of driving back to reykjavík every night. • iceland’s weather changes fast. always check the forecast and road conditions before heading out. • pack waterproof layers. the wind is no joke. • bring a reusable water bottle, the tap water is some of the best you’ll ever drink. • give yourself flexibility. the best memories usually happen when you aren’t rushing to the next stop. 👍 pros: ✔️ one of the safest countries i’ve visited ✔️ incredible scenery around every corner ✔️ easy road trip destination ✔️ amazing geothermal spas ✔️ perfect for adventure lovers 👎 cons: • it’s expensive. • weather can change by the hour. • you’ll wish you had booked a longer trip. have you been to iceland? if so, what would you add to this list? 🇮🇸👇 iceland reykjavik icelandtravel traveltips bucketlisttravel practical iceland travel tips and a must-do 5–7 day itinerary: reykjavík, golden circle, south coast, jökulsárlón, geothermal lagoons, packing and driving advice. iceland traveltips thinking about visiting iceland? below are the practical tips and a must-do 5–7 day itinerary to help you plan a relaxed, memorable trip.  must-do itinerary (recommended 5–7 days) - reykjavík: start in the capital to explore cafés, museums, and walkable streets. you don’t need a rental car for the city itself. - golden circle: visit þingvellir, geysir area, and gullfoss. start early or drive the loop in reverse to avoid tour buses. - south coast: waterfalls, black sand beaches, and dramatic cliffs. stay overnight on the south coast instead of returning to reykjavík the same day. - jökulsárlón glacier lagoon & diamond beach: a highlight worth allowing extra time for photography and walks near the ice. - a geothermal lagoon: book one — sky lagoon is highly recommended for its location and facilities.  insider tips for a smoother trip - trip length: stay at least 5–7 days. trying to cram everything into fewer days reduces flexibility and enjoyment. - car rental timing: rent your car when leaving reykjavík. the city is easy to explore on foot or by public transport. - beat the crowds: start the golden circle early or do it in reverse to avoid large tour buses. - overnight strategy: base at least one night on the south coast to see more without long returns to reykjavík. - weather and road checks: iceland’s weather changes fast. always check the forecast and road conditions before heading out. - pack smart: waterproof layers are essential — the wind and spray can be intense. bring sturdy shoes for uneven terrain. - tap water and sustainability: bring a reusable water bottle; icelandic tap water is exceptionally good. - flexibility: allow buffer time in your schedule. many of the best memories come from unplanned stops and slower pacing.  pros and cons - pros:   • one of the safest countries to visit   • breathtaking scenery at nearly every stop   • straightforward road-trip routes   • excellent geothermal spas and lagoons   • ideal for adventure lovers - cons:   • travel costs are high   • weather can change hourly   • you may wish you booked a longer trip  final takeaway plan for 5–7 days, rent a car outside reykjavík, prioritize an overnight on the south coast, and pack waterproof layers. book a geothermal lagoon experience like sky lagoon and leave room for spontaneous stops — those relaxed moments often become the best memories.  what would you add to this list from your iceland visit?

to ever find it. the summer of 1627 proved otherwise. darkhistory forgottenhistory ottomanempire iceland  a 1627 barbary raid seized nearly 400 icelanders; guðrún símonardóttir spent nine years enslaved in algiers before ransom returned her. read her story and the tyrkjaránið aftermath. darkhistory forgottenhistory ottomanempire iceland lead in july 1627 three ships carrying barbary corsairs under ottoman authority appeared off the westman islands. icelanders, believing their remote island safe from foreign attack, were caught unprepared. in a three‑day raid led by murat reis, the settlement was burned, between thirty and fifty people were killed, and nearly 250 were marched to the ships. among those taken was a young woman, guðrún símonardóttir.  the raid and its immediate aftermath the ships moved through the settlement quickly and destructively. homes and the church were set on fire as captors rounded up residents. survivors faced massacre, displacement, or capture. the event became known in iceland as tyrkjaránið — the turkish abductions — reflecting the origin of the corsairs as north african seafarers operating from algiers under ottoman influence.  the markets of algiers captives were carried south to algiers and sold in slave markets. roughly thirty of the transported icelanders died within the first month of captivity. those who survived were auctioned. guðrún símonardóttir was among those sold and spent the next nine years enslaved in algiers, cut off from home and uncertain if rescue would come.  ransom and return it took nine years for a ransom expedition to reach algiers and free some of the captives. guðrún was among the group eventually returned to iceland. she came back to an island physically and emotionally scarred, having last seen it in flames.  life after captivity after her return guðrún remarried. her husband was hallgrímur pétursson, a young poet who later became one of iceland’s most celebrated religious poets. today hallgrímskirkja, the most recognizable church in reykjavík, bears his name — a notable connection few visitors realize: his wife had been enslaved in algiers for nine years before meeting him.  longer legacy nearly four hundred people were taken from iceland that summer of 1627. a ransom effort eventually freed around fifty; the remainder were never accounted for. the event shattered the island’s sense of isolation and remains a dark chapter in icelandic history.  takeaway and context the turkish abductions show how even remote communities could be swept into wider geopolitical and economic systems: barbary corsairs operating from algiers, sanctioned by ottoman power, conducting raids that fed mediterranean slave markets. guðrún símonardóttir’s story, from capture to nine years in captivity to eventual return and remarriage to hallgrímur pétursson, personalizes that history and links it to a lasting cultural landmark in reykjavík.  questions for readers what surprised you this guide collects the best food in iceland as recommended by locals and visitors so you can try a variety of icelandic dishes and top restaurants while in the capital.  what to expect from icelandic food icelandic cuisine highlights fresh seafood, grass-fed lamb, dairy-based specialties and bold traditional items. reykjavik offers everything from casual street food to refined seafood and modern takes on classic icelandic flavors.  top dishes to try - icelandic seafood: fresh cod, haddock, langoustine and arctic char are widely praised by locals and visitors. look for grilled or pan-seared preparations in reykjavik restaurants.  - lamb: icelandic lamb is known for its flavor; try roasted or slow-cooked lamb dishes at local bistros.  - traditional fare: skyr (thick dairy), rye breads, and classic preparations appear on many menus — sample them to experience authentic tastes.  - street food and snacks: fish and chips, open-faced rye sandwiches, and gourmet hot dogs are popular quick options in reykjavik.  where to eat in reykjavik (how to choose) - for seafood: choose waterfront or well-reviewed seafood restaurants offering fresh local catch and langoustine plates.  - for lamb and traditional dishes: look for neighborhood bistros and family-run kitchens that emphasize icelandic ingredients.  - for a quick local bite: try reykjavik street food stalls and the famous icelandic hot dog stands.  tips from locals and visitors - try a mix: sample both traditional dishes and modern interpretations to get a full sense of icelandic cuisine.  - ask staff for daily catches or specials — reykjavik chefs often feature fresh, seasonal seafood.  - share plates: ordering a few different dishes is a good way to taste more without over-ordering.  close / call to action which icelandic dish would you try first? reykjavík, the capital and largest city of iceland, is the world's northernmost sovereign state capital. home to roughly a third of the nation's population, it is the country's cultural, economic, and governmental hub and serves as the primary gateway for exploring iceland’s spectacular natural wonders. things to do: hallgrímskirkja: this striking, basalt-inspired lutheran church is the city's most iconic landmark. take the elevator to the top of the tower for panoramic views of the city's colorful rooftops and the surrounding sea. perlan (the pearl): located atop oskjuhlid hill, this glass dome features a rotating restaurant, an observation deck, and immersive indoor nature exhibits. harpa concert hall: a masterpiece of modern architecture on the waterfront, recognized by its distinctive honeycomb glass façade. geothermal swimming: visit local pools like laugardalslaug or the famous blue lagoon (located just outside the city) to relax in naturally heated, mineral-rich waters. whale watching: book a wildlife tour right from the old harbour to spot minke whales, humpbacks, and puffins. golden circle tours: use the city as a base camp to easily access the nearby wonders of the golden circle, including thingvellir national park, the geysir geothermal area, and the gullfoss waterfall. the sun voyager (sólfar): a beautiful viking ship sculpture located right on the waterfront that represents hope and freedom. the icelandic phallological museum:  more than 200 penises & penile parts representing almost all icelandic land & sea mammals. harpa concert hall: an architectural masterpiece at the old harbor, featuring a striking glass façade modeled on iceland's basalt columns northern lights in reykjavik: between late august and early april when the nights are dark enough. prime viewing requires clear skies and high solar activity. best spots-  grótta nature reserve perlan hill (öskjuhlíð) sun voyager sculpture grace explorepage✨ fy goviral reykjavik
